data.title: SpaceX's IPO debacle: Another lesson for the crypto world.
data.description:
Author: Little Bear Biscuit; Source: BitpushNews

On June 12th, SpaceX officially listed on Nasdaq.

How large? It raised approximately $75 billion, with a valuation reaching $1.75 trillion—the largest IPO in human history, bar none. The offering price was $135, and it took off immediately upon opening.

Traditional brokerages were scrambling to participate in this star stock. What about retail investors in the cryptocurrency market? They weren't originally eligible, but several CEXs said, "We have tokenized stocks (or direct connections to US stocks), you can participate in the IPO using USDT."

Major exchanges also did a lot of pre-launch hype and marketing, but what happened?

It all backfired.

It was a disaster.

The core conflict of the entire incident points to xStocks. On the day of the listing, xStocks' upstream underwriters were unable to deliver the full amount of SpaceX's underlying shares to participating platforms due to a significant reduction in retail allocations. Several exchanges, including Bybit and Binance, which partnered with xStocks, subsequently announced that they could not complete the settlement and would only issue collective refunds. Among the major centralized exchanges (CEXs), Gate may emerge as the biggest winner. According to its official announcement, Gate operates independently through its own IPO Access/Pre-IPOs channel and does not rely on third-party platforms such as xStocks. The total allocation was approximately 33,900 SPCX (worth approximately $20 million), distributed using a transparent rule that combines time weighting and capital allocation. Platform announcements indicate that after the subscription period ends (June 12th), the asset certificates are expected to be directly distributed to users' Gate Stock accounts around 14:00–15:00 UTC, supporting immediate trading. User feedback generally confirms that they "received the goods immediately," indicating a high rate of fulfillment. The following are the IPO subscription results and "cleanup" plans of other major cryptocurrency exchanges: Kraken received the most criticism from the community. It received far less allocation from underwriters than expected. While some user orders were filled, each person only received approximately 4.28 shares (worth about $600). Regardless of whether you were a large investor or a small investor, everyone was treated equally. Many users exclaimed, "It would have been better not to participate at all!" Binance: Full refunds, then personally invests $1 million to airdrop SPCXB tokens from the bStocks ecosystem to each participant (approximately 27,000), averaging about $37-40 per person. A smart move. Bybit: Zero distribution, full refund of principal, plus an extra 4 days of interest (11 yuan for $10,000). Not amazing, but not bad either. Bitget: Cancelled, full refund + small compensation such as gas vouchers, but the problem is that the refund is in USDT instead of the original asset, and the price difference is deducted. Also, a mention of OKX: OKX did not participate in this spot/IPO product, only providing derivative contracts, perfectly avoiding all the pitfalls. It has to be said, Lao Xu once again wrote "The Compliance Life" correctly :) Why can't retail investors in the crypto world get quotas? This mishap, on the surface, is that xStocks, the upstream service provider, "dropped the ball" at a crucial moment, but looking deeper, it is actually a "dimensional reduction attack" of the traditional financial compliance and clearing system on the crude tokenization narrative of the crypto world. US IPO allocation is a centralized game highly monopolized by the "lead underwriter" and driven by profit. According to SEC (Securities and Exchange Commission) underwriting regulations, new share allocations are tightly controlled by lead underwriters such as Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley. Top-tier licensed brokers like Fidelity, being part of the underwriting syndicate, directly receive first-hand orders and can naturally support retail investors in subscribing to new shares; while ordinary brokers not on the list can only obtain second or third-hand allocations. In extreme cases like SpaceX, where oversubscription is several times higher than expected, underwriters will only prioritize allocating to top institutions and core clients. The final asset transfer must be strictly cleared through the DTC (Depository & Clearing Corporation) and compliant accounts of licensed clearing brokers. Binance, Bybit, and other cryptocurrency exchanges are not licensed clearinghouses in the US stock market. The xStocks platform they connect to is essentially a middleman "grouping" transactions outside the compliance firewall. When traditional Wall Street's major funds are insufficient even for their own allocations, underwriters will directly cut off the quotas of these secondary channels. Without clearing sovereignty, these middlemen are not even qualified to enter the market in the face of Wall Street's top-level clearing walls, and downstream cryptocurrency exchanges are naturally forced to refund. Returning to the recent RWA (Rich Democracy and Investment) craze for tokenized US stocks, the current crypto market desperately craves anchoring itself to genuine, high-quality hard assets. However, this incident exposes a core problem: tokenized stocks ≠ you actually own the stocks. Legally, these tokenized products are "shadow derivatives." Where does the quota come from? How is it held in cross-border custody? How are the tokens mapped? How is it finally delivered? If any link in this chain goes wrong, retail investors can only wait for a refund. Even worse, your name won't appear on SpaceX's shareholder register. You have no voting rights, and dividends are not protected by Delaware corporate law. You think you're buying the future of spaceships, but you're actually just holding a worthless IOU that can be torn up at any time outside the high walls of compliance. If RWA truly wants to be implemented, it needs to connect these intermediary chains first. Otherwise, it will continue to pay the price for its "tuition fees." Blockchain wanting to penetrate traditional finance? It's probably still a long way off.

data.title: A major whale deposited all of its HPYE into Coinbase, making SpaceX the sixth largest company in the US.
data.description:
Headline

▌A Genesis Whale Has Deposited All 576,148 HYPE Tokens into Coinbase, Worth Approximately $35.28 Million

On June 13th, according to Onchain Lens monitoring, a Genesis Whale has deposited all 576,148 HYPE tokens (worth approximately $35.28 million) into Coinbase.

▌SpaceX's Market Value Surpasses $2 Trillion on First Day of Trading, Becoming the Sixth Largest Company in the US

On June 13th, SpaceX (SPCX.O) surged on its first day of trading on Nasdaq, surpassing a market capitalization of $2 trillion. The IPO was more stable than many observers had anticipated.

Trading in the stock began late Friday morning, with most of the session up 15% to 30% from Thursday's pricing, exhibiting relatively small volatility. It closed at approximately $161, up 19%, making the company the sixth-largest company in the U.S. The final settlement price is yet to be determined. The trade saw over 500 million shares traded, worth approximately $80 billion. With the large IPOs of Anthropic and OpenAI looming, market observers had worried that the influx of new shares could weigh on market performance after a prolonged period of relative IPO scarcity. However, investors of all types, from large institutions to retail fans of Musk, were in high spirits by the close of trading. "SpaceX not only broke records in terms of IPO fundraising, but also left other large companies far behind. Adding so much market capitalization in an instant when the initial valuation was already close to $2 trillion is impressive," said Dan Coatsworth, head of marketing at AJ Bell. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $61,480.92, up 1.1% in the last 24 hours; ETH price is $1,603.27, up 1.4% in the last 24 hours; BNB price is $583.11, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours; SOL price is $64.08, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours. DOGE price is $0.08340, 24-hour change +1.5%; XRP price is $1.13, 24-hour change +0.9%; TRX price is $0.3251, 24-hour change +1.6%; WLFI price is $0.05678, 24-hour change +2.0%; ...5678, 24-hour change +2.0%; WLFI price is $0.05678, 24-hour change +2.0%; WLFI price is $0.05678, 24-hour change +2.0%; WLFI price is $0.05678, 24-hour change +2.0%; WLFI price is $0.05678, 24-hour change +2.0%; WLFI price is $0.05678, 24-hour change +2.0%; WLFI price is $0.0 HYPE price is $59.41, a 24-hour change of +5.2%. Policy ▌SpaceX reaches rare deal with investment banks, IPO greenshoe option fee is zero. On June 13, SpaceX reached a rare deal with investment banks, the IPO greenshoe option fee is zero.

▌US CFTC Sues New Mexico, Continuing its Fight for Sports Betting Market Regulation Power

On June 13, the US CFTC filed a lawsuit in the Federal District Court for the District of New Mexico against Governor Michelle Lujan Grisham, Attorney General Raúl Torrez, and other officials, attempting to prevent the state from applying gambling regulations to prediction market platforms. Previously, New Mexico sued Kalshi, accusing it of offering sports betting to state residents without a license and allowing users under the state's legal gambling age of 21 to participate. The New Mexico Attorney General stated that legal gambling in the state can only operate under tribal and state gambling agreements or strict state regulations. The CFTC, however, argues that platforms like Kalshi offer federally regulated derivatives contracts, not gambling products under state law. CFTC Chairman Michael Selig stated that New Mexico is attempting to impose its state betting laws on federal derivatives exchanges under the exclusive jurisdiction of the CFTC. In recent months, the CFTC has filed lawsuits against several states, including Wisconsin, Illinois, Arizona, Connecticut, and New York, to establish its regulatory authority over the sports prediction market. This week, the agency also proposed broader prediction market rules, which would generally still allow sports-related contracts, indicating an escalating conflict between federal and state governments over the boundaries of prediction markets and sports betting.

▌ Attorneys General from Multiple US States Launch Joint Investigation into OpenAI

On June 13, according to sources, attorneys general from several US states have jointly launched an investigation into OpenAI.

Multiple sources familiar with the matter said that the artificial intelligence company received a broad subpoena on Friday, requiring it to submit various operational and user impact documents, covering advertising business, user activity and retention, consumer and health data management, business related to minors and the elderly, deep learning models, model flattery issues, and internal company rules and regulations. An OpenAI spokesperson stated, "Artificial intelligence is an emerging and powerful technology, and we are committed to ensuring the public can safely enjoy the benefits of technology in a compliant and secure manner. We value the concerns raised by state attorneys general and will actively cooperate with relevant departments." This month, the company secretly filed initial public offering (IPO) documents with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. (Sina Finance) (Cailian Press) Blockchain Applications ▌Blockworks Acquires Crypto Data Platform Messari for Over $10 Million On June 13th, according to the Wall Street Journal, crypto data service provider Blockworks acquired competitor Messari for over $10 million. The company was valued at approximately $300 million in 2022, representing a significant discount in this transaction. The report points out that the deal reflects the survival pressure faced by once highly valued startups amidst a deepening crypto bear market. Founded in 2018, Blockworks initially focused on media and events, later expanding into news, research, and data businesses. Last October, it closed its news division, shifting its focus to data, investor relations, and compliance services. The company recently completed an extension of its funding round, valuing the company at approximately $192 million. Cryptocurrency

According to CoinDesk, the U.S. Department of Commerce recently signed a letter of intent to invest over $2 billion in nine quantum computing companies to build quantum computer infrastructure capable of breaking Bitcoin, Ethereum, and mainstream internet encryption. IBM will receive $1 billion to build a quantum-level wafer fab, GlobalFoundries will receive $375 million, and the remaining funds will be allocated to seven quantum hardware companies.

▌The largest SPCX long position on Hyperliquid has essentially closed out its SPCX long positions, incurring a loss of $300,000.

On June 13th, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin's monitoring, the largest SPCX long position on Hyperliquid has essentially closed out its SPCX long positions, ultimately incurring a loss of $300,000 instead of making a profit. Yesterday, before SPCX's listing, a total of $36.4 million worth of SPCX long positions were opened on Hyperliquid, with an average price of $171.4. After the listing, the SPCX price hovered around this price without further increase, and the investor eventually closed out its positions over the past few hours at an average price of $169.8, resulting in a loss of $300,000.

... **Important Economic Developments** On June 13, Israeli Defense Minister Katz issued a statement on June 12, saying that Israel must ensure it has the "capability to take independent action to prevent Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons." He and Prime Minister Netanyahu have instructed the Israeli military to prepare accordingly. The Times of Israel believes that Katz's remarks suggest that Israel may again use force to prevent Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons. In his statement, Katz said that US President Trump is pushing for an agreement with Iran based on US interests, including the shared interest of preventing Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons. Katz said, "We expect him (Trump) to adhere to this principle, and also to adhere to principles on Iran's missile program and the issue of 'terrorist proxies.'" The statement also stated that Israel will not withdraw its troops from the so-called "safe zones" it occupies in Lebanon, Syria, and the Gaza Strip. The statement said that Israel's security principle is to address "threats from both near and far" simultaneously, "rather than compromise and concession." (Xinhua)

▌US Treasury traders lower their bets on a 2026 Fed rate hike

On June 13, as market optimism grew regarding the end of the war with Iran, US Treasury traders bet that the Federal Reserve would raise interest rates early next year as the week drew to a close. On Friday afternoon, interest rate swap transactions showed that traders now expect a 100% probability of a 25 basis point rate hike by January 2027; previously, they had bet on a rate hike this year. This shift came after data showed that US core inflation accelerated at a relatively moderate pace last month, US crude oil prices fell below $85 a barrel, and US President Donald Trump declared progress on a peace agreement with Iran.

“Signs of a possible reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and falling oil prices have been key narratives driving the market,” said Dhiraj Narula, U.S. interest rate strategist at HSBC Securities. “These factors have, to some extent, delayed expectations of when interest rate hikes will occur.” U.S. bank deposits fell to $19.296 trillion from $19.338 trillion last week.

▌Sources: US Military Shoots Down Multiple Iranian One-Way Attack Drones Heading Towards the Strait of Hormuz

June 13, according to Reuters: A source revealed that the US military shot down multiple Iranian one-way attack drones heading towards the Strait of Hormuz. The source stated that Iranian drones posed a threat to commercial shipping. (Jinshi)

    Golden Encyclopedia

    ▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" Amid Geopolitical Crises?

    Every time a geopolitical crisis occurs, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves.

    For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: In-depth analysis of SpaceX's IPO: Is the company worth $2.5 trillion?
data.description:
I completely missed last year's commercial space boom because my first job was at an institute of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, working on satellite R&D. Although I later switched to semiconductors, I continued to follow this area, believing progress wouldn't be as rapid, but the market experienced a major boom. Today, SpaceX is about to go public. Although Elon Musk is known for his outlandish pronouncements, SpaceX already has mature technology and a market presence. The offering price is $135 per share, and Oppenheimer has given it a target price of $190, corresponding to a company valuation of approximately $2.5 trillion. Many people will ask: Is this company really worth that much money?

To answer this question, we need to understand what SpaceX is doing. This is not an ordinary space company, nor simply a satellite communications service provider, nor just another AI startup. SpaceX is attempting to establish dominance in three areas simultaneously: launch services, global satellite communications, and artificial intelligence.

Moreover, these three businesses are not simply pieced together, but rather a meticulously designed system, with each part supporting the others. In a recent interview, Elon Musk spent considerable time discussing the "Kardashev Scale"—a framework for measuring the progress of civilizations using energy utilization capabilities. This framework divides civilizations into three levels: Type I civilizations can utilize the energy of an entire planet, Type II civilizations can utilize the energy of a star, and Type III civilizations can utilize the energy of an entire galaxy. What is humanity's current state? We are far from even reaching Type I civilization. Earth receives only about one five-hundred-millionth of the sun's total output, and the solar energy we utilize is less than one trillionth of that. The sun accounts for 99.86% of the solar system's mass; of the remaining 0.14%, most is Jupiter. On this scale, Earth is merely a "tiny speck of dust." Musk's point is straightforward: if humanity wants to make progress at the Kardashev scale, it must go to space. Utilizing even a millionth of the sun's energy requires deploying massive amounts of solar energy collection facilities in space. To achieve this, three prerequisites are needed: large-scale launch capacity, energy collection and cooling systems in space, and a sufficient number of computing chips. This is the underlying logic of SpaceX's three main businesses. Starship addresses the launch capacity issue, aiming to send millions of tons of supplies into orbit annually. Starlink and space-based AI data centers address energy utilization by deploying solar panels and computing facilities in space. The Terafab chip factory addresses chip supply, as current global chip production capacity is far from sufficient. This sounds like science fiction, but SpaceX is gradually turning it into reality. Business Segment One: Starship SpaceX currently accounts for 85-90% of the world's total orbital mass. This number is astonishing in itself, but more importantly, it represents the transformative power of Starship. The Falcon 9 has already proven the commercial value of reusable rockets. It reduced launch costs from $25,000/kg during the Space Shuttle era to approximately $2,700/kg. But Starship aims for $100/kg—another order of magnitude reduction. Starship is the largest, heaviest, and most powerful spacecraft in human history. Its thrust is 2-3 times that of the Saturn V (depending on the version), and it is the first super-heavy rocket designed to be fully reusable. SpaceX even eliminated the weight of the landing legs, allowing the launch tower to directly capture the returning rocket. This extreme quality optimization aims to achieve one goal: to make rockets like airplanes, able to land, refuel, and take off again. The target operational rhythm is one launch per hour, and SpaceX can now manufacture one Starship every two weeks. Once the Gigabay factory in Texas is operational, annual production capacity will reach 1,000 ships. Why is such a high launch frequency necessary? Because SpaceX plans to deploy not a few hundred satellites, but hundreds of thousands. Starlink V3 satellites are larger and more powerful, and each Starship can carry approximately 60. To deploy 100,000 V3 satellites, even with 60 launches per launch, approximately 1,700 launches would be needed. To complete deployment within a few years, multiple launches per day would be required. More importantly, without Starship, SpaceX's other ambitious plans cannot be realized. The space AI data center needs to send a large number of solar panels, radiators, and computing devices into orbit. The lunar base needs to transport construction materials. Mars colonization is entirely dependent on the success of Starship. Starship's commercialization target is the end of 2026, but this timeline is already several years behind schedule. Recent test flights have made significant progress, but there are still some issues to be resolved with the rocket and boosters. This is the cornerstone of the entire strategy, and any further delays will affect all subsequent plans. Business Segment Two: Starlink If Starship is SpaceX's future, then Starlink is its current cash cow. As of 2025, Starlink had over 10,000 satellites in orbit, serving 10 million users and generating $11.4 billion in annual revenue. This figure was only $3.9 billion in 2023, nearly doubling in two years. More importantly, this business has demonstrated a powerful flywheel effect. Each new satellite increases the network's total capacity, enabling it to serve more users. The marginal cost of adding users is extremely low—the satellites are already in orbit, and serving an additional user incurs almost no additional cost. User payments become cash flow, which is then reinvested in research and development and the deployment of better satellites. Better satellites attract more users, accelerating the flywheel's rotation. Currently, Starlink's cost is approximately $20 per user per month, with a goal of reducing it to $10. This would bring it to half the price of traditional service providers. Moreover, with the deployment of V3 satellites, service quality will reach or even surpass fiber optic levels. Each V3 satellite can provide over 1Tbps of capacity, several times that of current satellites. SpaceX plans to eventually deploy 100,000 V3/V4/V5 satellites, ten times the current number. This will make Starlink a core part of the global internet infrastructure. Musk even mentioned that Starlink may eventually dominate internet, cloud, and AI traffic and charge for unique services such as interconnectivity. Even greater ambitions lie in the mobile market. SpaceX plans to launch a hybrid satellite-and-terrestrial network within three years, enabling direct-to-cell connections between mobile phones and satellites. This requires massive spectrum investment—estimated at around $100 billion—and significantly increased capital expenditures. By 2030, Starlink's annual CapEx could reach $35 billion. Oppenheimer predicts that Starlink will eventually capture 25% of the $2 trillion communications market and have 230 million broadband users within ten years. Whether this prediction is accurate remains to be seen, but the direction is clear: Starlink is transforming from a satellite internet service provider into a dominant player in global communications infrastructure. It's worth noting that SpaceX is currently the only company with experience operating tens of thousands of satellites. This experience cannot be accumulated overnight; it involves a series of complex systems engineering issues such as orbit management, collision avoidance, satellite updates, and ground station coordination. This is a natural competitive advantage. Business Segment Three: The Bold Gamble with xAI In early 2026, SpaceX acquired xAI for $250 billion. This price itself demonstrates Musk's emphasis on the AI ​​business. xAI is not simply an AI model developer, but a vertically integrated system attempting to cover the entire AI stack. xAI consists of six parts: X (data source), Grok (large language models), Colossus (ground data center), Cursor (AI programming tools), Terafab (chip manufacturing), and Macrohard (software system). SpaceX is about to acquire Cursor for $60 billion; this AI programming tool has tripled its revenue in the past six months, reaching an annualized revenue of $3 billion. However, the challenges facing xAI are also obvious: the capabilities of Grok models are currently about six months behind those of major competitors. Musk has publicly acknowledged this gap and stated that it needs to be caught up. In the rapidly evolving field of AI, a six-month gap can mean a lot. What is SpaceX's strategy? In the short term, it will continue to use cutting-edge models from other vendors. In the medium term, it will gain market share and user data through products like Cursor. In the long term, it will establish a leading position based on cost and capacity advantages. The core of this long-term advantage is the space-based AI data center. The AI1 satellite is SpaceX's first-generation product designed for space computing. Its specifications are clear: a peak power of 150 kilowatts and a continuous power of 120 kilowatts, equivalent to an Nvidia GB300 rack equipped with 72 GPUs. The satellite's main components are solar panels, heat sinks, and computing chips. The solar panels are designed for 250 watts per square meter, and the heat sink is designed for 1,400 watts per square meter (double-sided cooling), with a wingspan of approximately 70 meters. These technologies are already in use or will be used in Starlink V3 satellites, requiring no magical technological breakthroughs. In fact, the AI1 satellite is simpler than Starlink satellites. Starlink requires complex phased array antennas, parabolic antennas, and numerous laser links. The AI1 satellite mainly consists of solar panels, heat sinks, and chips, connecting to the Starlink constellation via laser links, from which Starlink transmits data back to Earth. What are the core advantages of a space-based data center? First, energy. Solar energy is 10 times more efficient in space than on Earth—there is no atmosphere to obstruct it, no day-night cycle, and no weather effects. Second, heat dissipation. Ground-based data centers require complex cooling systems, while in space, heat can be directly radiated into the vacuum. Third, latency. At an orbital altitude of 600-800 kilometers, the latency for light-speed transmission is only about 3 milliseconds, which is perfectly acceptable. Of course, technological challenges remain. Chips need to operate reliably in the space environment, cooling systems need to be validated, and costs need to be proven competitive. SpaceX's timeline is: reaching an annualized rate of 1 gigawatt per year by the end of 2025, 10 gigawatts per year by mid-2027, 100 gigawatts per year by mid-2028, with a long-term goal of 1 terawatt per year. For comparison, the current global cloud computing capacity is approximately 100 gigawatts. SpaceX's goal is to deploy computing power equivalent to 10 times the current global capacity within a few years—and in space. What if the progress of the space data center is not as expected? SpaceX has contingency plans. It has already built the Colossus terrestrial data center, reportedly the world's fastest and lowest-cost data center. Before space data center technology matures, Colossus can support the development of AI businesses. How many chips are needed to achieve 1 terawatt of computing power? Assuming each chip provides 1 kilowatt, that's 1 billion chips. This far exceeds the current global chip production capacity. This is the reason for Terafab's existence. This planned chip factory will cover 100 million square feet, 10 times the size of Tesla's Texas Gigafactory. It will be built in partnership with Tesla and Intel, with a target annual production capacity of 1 billion chips. Construction of Terafab has already begun. A solar panel manufacturing facility in Bastrop, Texas, is under construction, and an AI satellite production facility is about to begin construction. SpaceX expects to achieve reasonable production capacity by the end of 2025. However, even if Terafab is successfully built, it will only support terawatts. To increase capacity by another 1,000 times to petawatts, a completely different approach is needed. SpaceX's lunar program sounds like science fiction, but the technological logic is clear. Solar panels and heat sinks can be produced on-site on the moon, and chips can be transported from Earth or manufactured on the moon. What are the advantages of the moon? Gravity is only one-sixth that of Earth, and there is no atmosphere. This means that AI satellites can be accelerated to sufficient speeds using electromagnetic mass actuators—essentially giant linear motors—and launched directly into deep space without rockets. This method is far more efficient than launching from Earth. And once a production base is established on the Moon, scaling becomes relatively easy. This is the only viable path from terawatts to petawatts. Of course, this plan hinges on the success of Starship, the space data center, Terafab, and the establishment of an industrial base on the Moon. Every step is challenging, but every step is on SpaceX's roadmap. SpaceX's core competitiveness lies not only in technological innovation but also in its manufacturing capabilities. Over 80% of Starship components, Starlink satellites, and related equipment are manufactured in-house. This extreme vertical integration is uncommon in modern business, but it gives SpaceX a significant advantage: cost control, quality control, rapid iteration, and supply chain security. xAI is the only fully vertically integrated AI developer. From data on the X platform to models in Grok, to data centers in Colossus, to the future Terafab chip, and software systems in Macrohard, the entire stack is under SpaceX's control. This control is not just for profit, but to ensure that scarce resources—chips, data, and computing power—are prioritized for SpaceX's strategic goals. The Gigafactory in Bastrop, Texas, is expanding. Starlink user terminals are already being produced here, with an expected final output of hundreds of millions of units. A solar panel manufacturing facility is under construction, and an AI satellite production facility is about to begin construction. SpaceX aims to achieve reasonable production capacity by the end of 2025 to support large-scale deployments starting in 2026. SpaceX's engineering philosophy is the Silicon Valley-style "fail fast" approach: test to break, learn from failure, and iterate rapidly. Starlink has already reached version V3, and Starship is constantly being improved. This approach is not common in the traditional aerospace industry, but it allows SpaceX to advance technology at a much faster pace than its competitors. The competitive landscape is virtually unassailable. SpaceX's absolute dominance in the launch market is virtually unchallenged. It accounts for 85-90% of global orbital mass, with the remaining 10-15% mainly from China, and other countries and companies combined accounting for only 5-7%. The most likely competitor is Blue Origin. The New Glenn rocket's maiden flight was successful, but a major accident occurred subsequently, damaging the launch pad. Blue Origin stated it would repair the damage within seven months and resume flights by the end of 2026, but this timeline is considered quite aggressive. More importantly, this accident severely impacted Blue Origin's Kuiper satellite deployment plans. Kuiper is Amazon's satellite internet project, originally planned to launch a certain number of satellites by July 2026, a deadline that will now certainly be unattainable. Industry experts generally believe that Kuiper will need 3-4 years to become truly competitive in the satellite network market. Over the next 3-4 years, Starlink will continue to expand. By the time Kuiper truly reaches scale, Starlink may already hold a dominant market share, boasting tens of millions of users and a comprehensive service system. It will become much more difficult for newcomers to catch up. The small and medium-sized launch market still offers opportunities. Companies like Rocket Lab, focusing on small payloads, have established themselves in this segment. The European polar orbit launch market also has room for growth, as geographical limitations make equatorial launches difficult for European countries. However, in the heavy launch market, SpaceX's lead is likely to persist for a long time. While the US government wants to maintain competition (not putting all eggs in one basket), there are simply too few truly competitive players. The Overwhelming Advantages of Satellite Communications In the field of satellite communications, Starlink's advantages are even more pronounced. It is currently the only company with experience operating tens of thousands of satellites. This kind of experience cannot be accumulated overnight. Managing 10,000 satellites is an extremely complex systems engineering problem. It requires real-time tracking of the position and status of each satellite, orbit planning to avoid collisions, coordinating communications at ground stations, and managing satellite upgrades and replacements. SpaceX has built a complete system to handle these problems, and this system is constantly being improved. Even if Kuiper can overcome the launch delay problem, it will take time to build similar operational capabilities. In the meantime, Starlink will continue to improve its services, reduce costs, and expand its user base. Network effects amplify the advantage of leaders. In the field of AI, SpaceX is currently in a follower position. Its Grok model lags behind its main competitors by about six months, a significant gap in the rapidly evolving AI landscape. However, SpaceX possesses several unique advantages. First is the data on the X platform. Social media data is invaluable for training AI models, and X is one of the few platforms with massive amounts of real-time data. Second is the acquisition of Cursor. Cursor has experienced rapid growth in the AI ​​programming tools market, tripling its revenue in six months. This has brought not only revenue but also users and usage data. The most crucial advantage is future computing power. If the space data center is successfully deployed, SpaceX will possess the world's largest and lowest-cost computing power. In the computationally-intensive field of AI, this could be a decisive advantage. Of course, all of this hinges on the success of the space data center. If insurmountable technical or economic obstacles arise, SpaceX's AI strategy will require significant adjustments. However, even relying solely on its ground-based Colossus data center, SpaceX is capable of securing a place in the AI ​​market. Financial Performance SpaceX's financial data demonstrates a rapidly growing, highly capital-intensive business model. Revenue growth is significant. $10.4 billion in 2023, $14 billion in 2024 (35% growth), and $18.7 billion in 2025 (33% growth). Oppenheimer predicts it will reach $31.3 billion in 2026 (68% growth) and $58.8 billion in 2027 (88% growth). The business structure is rapidly changing. In 2025, Starlink accounted for 61% of revenue ($11.4 billion), launch services for 22% ($4.1 billion), and AI for 17% ($3.2 billion). By 2026, AI revenue is expected to grow by 324% to $13.7 billion, increasing its share to 44%. After 2027, AI revenue may surpass Starlink to become the largest revenue source. However, this growth requires huge investments. Capital expenditures are projected to reach $20.7 billion in 2025 and $59.9 billion in 2026, nearly tripling. Of this $59.9 billion, $49.9 billion will be invested in AI infrastructure, including space data centers, Terafab, and terrestrial data centers. Future capital needs are even more staggering. Oppenheimer estimates that achieving SpaceX's strategic goals will require approximately $1.6 trillion in cumulative capital expenditures and spectrum investments. This includes Starlink's expansion (particularly the $100 billion spectrum investment needed to enter the mobile market), Starship's mass production, the deployment of space data centers, the construction of Terafab, and more. SpaceX will also need an additional $300 billion in financing. An IPO is an important step, but far from enough. Multiple rounds of financing, bond issuance, and even mergers with Tesla or other companies may be necessary to raise more capital in the future. Regarding profitability, Oppenheimer predicts that SpaceX's revenue will exceed $200 billion by 2030, with an EBITDA margin of approximately 60%. By 2035, revenue could reach $900 billion, with EBITDA around $500 billion. These figures seem impressive, but they are based on the assumption that SpaceX can successfully execute its strategy. The Justification for $2.5 Trillion: Oppenheimer's target price of $190 corresponds to a company valuation of approximately $2.5 trillion, representing a 41% upside from the IPO price of $135. How was this valuation derived? Oppenheimer used a 10-year DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model with a weighted average cost of capital (WACC) of 10.5% and a long-term growth rate of 5%. The model predicts that SpaceX's revenue will be approximately $900 billion, EBITDA approximately $500 billion, and terminal EBIT $220 billion by 2035. The 10.5% WACC reflects the high risks facing SpaceX. Technological, execution, market, and regulatory risks are all significant. However, if SpaceX succeeds, the returns will be enormous. The reasonableness of this valuation depends on several key assumptions: First, the market potential for the three main business segments is indeed huge. Oppenheimer estimates that by 2035, the communications market will be around $2 trillion, the AI ​​market could reach trillions of dollars, and the space economy around $800 billion. The three combined exceed $10 trillion. If SpaceX can capture a significant share of these markets, a valuation of $2.5 trillion is not unreasonable. Secondly, space infrastructure has structural cost advantages. Solar energy is 10 times more efficient in space than on Earth, heat dissipation is easier, there are no land costs, and no environmental restrictions. If these advantages can be translated into actual cost advantages, SpaceX will have a unique competitive edge in the AI ​​computing market. Thirdly, the value created by vertical integration. SpaceX controls the entire chain from rocket manufacturing, satellite production, chip manufacturing to data center operations. This control not only reduces costs but also ensures supply chain security and rapid technological iteration. Fourth, Starlink's cash flow support capability. Starlink is already a profitable business and is growing rapidly. Its cash flow can support the development of Starship and xAI, forming a self-reinforcing cycle. However, its valuation also faces scrutiny. The biggest problem is execution risk. Starship's commercialization has been delayed for several years, the space data center is not yet validated, Terafab is still in the planning stage, and AI model capabilities are still catching up. Any significant delay or failure in any of these areas could have a ripple effect on the overall strategy. Another issue is the complexity of valuing the three businesses together. Launch services, satellite communications, and AI are three completely different businesses with different risk characteristics and growth prospects. Valuing them together makes it difficult for investors to accurately assess the value of each component. There are also corporate governance issues. SpaceX is highly dependent on Elon Musk, who also manages Tesla, SpaceX, and other companies. The limited number of shares in circulation (only 3.5%) could lead to significant stock price volatility. Public market scrutiny could influence SpaceX's consistently aggressive decision-making style. Several factors will determine the future. The commercialization of Starship is the most critical variable. Starship is the cornerstone of the entire strategy; without it, Starlink V3 cannot be deployed, space data centers cannot be realized, and a lunar base is out of the question. The current timeline is to enter commercial service by the end of 2026. Recent test flights have yielded significant progress—the rocket successfully launched, entered orbit, re-entered orbit, and landed, and the booster was successfully captured by the launch pad. However, some issues remain to be addressed, including certain systems of the rocket and boosters. If Starship can be commercialized on schedule and achieve high-frequency launches in the following years, SpaceX's strategy will proceed as planned. If there are significant delays—such as a postponement to 2028 or later—all subsequent plans will need to be adjusted, and the valuation will face pressure. Key metrics to watch include: the frequency and success rate of test flights, the start date of commercial launches, the rate of increase in launch frequency, and the rate of decrease in cost per launch. Validation of Space Data Centers Space AI data centers are the most innovative and controversial part of SpaceX's strategy. Technically, key issues include the effectiveness of the cooling system, the reliable operation of chips in the space environment, and competitive cost. SpaceX plans to reach an annualized rate of 1 gigawatt per year by the end of 2025, which will be the first important validation point. If initial validation is successful, the 10 gigawatt target by mid-2027 and the 100 gigawatt target by mid-2028 will become more credible. If technical or economic obstacles are encountered, SpaceX may need more time to resolve the issues or adjust its strategy, relying more on ground-based data centers. Key metrics to watch include: the launch time and number of AI1 satellites, preliminary test results, cost data (if released), and contracts signed with large enterprise clients. The Grok model is currently about six months behind its competitors, and this gap needs to be narrowed. The acquisition of Cursor will enhance SpaceX's AI product competitiveness in the short term. Cursor is growing rapidly in the AI ​​programming tools market, which itself is expanding rapidly. If the integration goes smoothly, SpaceX will gain a strong foothold in AI applications. In the long term, SpaceX needs to prove it can develop competitive AI models. This requires not only substantial computing resources (which is SpaceX's strength), but also an excellent research team, high-quality training data, and effective training methods. Key metrics to watch include: the release and performance evaluation of new versions of Grok, Cursor's user and revenue growth, the signing of large enterprise AI contracts, and SpaceX's AI talent recruitment efforts. Starlink's Continued Expansion is currently SpaceX's most stable source of cash flow, and its continued growth is crucial to the overall strategy. The deployment of V3 satellites will be a significant milestone. Each V3 satellite has several times the capacity of current satellites and will achieve fiber-optic performance. If V3 deployment proceeds smoothly, Starlink's service quality and capacity will be significantly improved. Entering the mobile market is another crucial step. This requires significant investment in spectrum and technology development, but the potential market size is far larger than that of fixed broadband. If SpaceX can establish a foothold in the mobile market, both revenue and subscriber numbers will increase substantially. Key metrics to watch include: the V3 satellite launch schedule, subscriber growth, ARPU (average revenue per user) changes, service quality improvements, and the timeline for entering the mobile market. Terafab Construction Progress To achieve 1 terawatt of computing power, SpaceX needs its own chip supply. Terafab is a key part of this strategy. Building a 100 million square foot factory is a massive undertaking in itself. Site selection, design, construction, equipment procurement, recruitment, and capacity ramp-up—each step requires time and capital. Moreover, chip manufacturing is a highly complex process, and improving yield rates can take years. Currently, Bastrop's solar panel manufacturing facility is under construction, which is a positive sign. However, it could take several years from groundbreaking to achieving meaningful production capacity. Key metrics to monitor include: factory construction progress, equipment procurement and installation, the production time of the first chips, yield rates, and capacity ramp-up speed. The development of emerging space industries: SpaceX's long-term value lies not only in its three current businesses but also in the emerging industries it may initiate. A lunar economy is a clear direction. If SpaceX can significantly reduce the cost of Earth-Moon transportation, lunar scientific research, resource development, and even tourism could become a reality. Oppenheimer conservatively estimates that the space economy will reach $800 billion by 2035, but this figure could increase significantly if the lunar and Martian economies develop smoothly. Point-to-point rocket transportation is another possibility. Starship could theoretically transport cargo or passengers to anywhere on Earth within an hour. If this service can be commercialized, it will create an entirely new market. Concepts such as on-orbit manufacturing, space energy production, and asteroid mining currently seem far-fetched, but they may gradually become feasible as launch costs decrease. These emerging industries currently contribute little to the valuation, but they represent SpaceX's long-term potential.

data.title: Sociological Reflections on the Restraints of AI-Powered Authoritarianism
data.description:
When artificial intelligence's capabilities cross a certain critical threshold, public discussion about its future splits into two extreme scenarios: one is a science fiction apocalyptic view of silicon-based life replacing carbon-based life, and the other is a vaguely emerging scenario where a tiny minority of technical experts and top-tier capital jointly control silicon-based technology, thereby imposing unprecedentedly sophisticated discipline and resource exploitation on all of humanity.

In 2024, Pope Francis addressed the G7 summit, calling for the use of religious and ethical "conscience" to constrain the direction of AI development, warning that "algorithms should not become tools of technocratic rule." That same year, several AI pioneers, including Geoffrey Hinton and Yoshua Bengio, published an open letter urging colleagues and companies "not to betray the original intention of science to serve the well-being of humanity" (Future Life Institute, 2023).

On the streets, from San Francisco to London, protesters held signs that read "AI Doesn't Belong to Silicon Valley Oligarchs," expressing a sense of angry powerlessness. From religious authorities to democratic systems to the general public, people are being reduced to passive prayers, urging a few technological "prophets" not to do evil. However, history has repeatedly proven that faith, preaching, and even individual conscience have negligible restraining effects in the face of structural power and enormous interests. A more relevant precedent comes from the nuclear weapons era: after the atomic bomb was invented and used to bomb Japan, witnessing its terrifying power and the control possessed by a single nation, it wasn't ethical appeals from politicians or academia, but rather a group of scientists directly involved in the Manhattan Project who risked treason by leaking core technologies to the Soviet Union. The resulting US-Soviet nuclear balance of terror, what Kissinger called the "Balance of Terror," ensured that for nearly eighty years after the Hiroshima and Nagasaki atomic bombings, no direct nuclear war broke out between nuclear-armed states. Even during the Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962 and the Soviet threat of a "surgical strike" against Chinese nuclear facilities in 1969, the fear of mutual assured destruction suppressed the impulse to attack. This reveals a cold sociological mechanism: only when power is countered by another power capable of inflicting devastating damage can the check and balance of power curb the abuse of power. Today, the level of centralization in AI technology far surpasses that of any general-purpose technology in history. A few large companies—actually, a tiny fraction of their top technical experts and managers—control the computing power, data, talent, and specialized hardware supply chain for training cutting-edge models, their capabilities permeating the very nerve endings of society. Given this situation, are top AI experts using "leaks" to implement checks and balances? How are governments intervening in advance? Can international law effectively limit AI? And can blockchain technology, originating from the cypherpunk movement, with its core principle of "decentralization," be translated into a sociological tool of checks and balances to break the AI ​​monopoly and create a kind of "algorithmic balance of terror"? I. The Concentration of Technological Power: From Surveillance Capitalism to Algorithmic Leviathan To understand the urgency of checks and balances, we must first confront reality. Currently, the concentration in the AI ​​field is manifested in a complex monopoly of "computing power—data—talent—capital." The computing power cost of training a single ultra-large-scale model (such as GPT 4o or Gemini Ultra) has reached hundreds of millions of dollars, making it difficult for any public research institution or sovereign state without top-tier capital support to participate in core innovation, let alone small and medium-sized enterprises and individual geeks. At the data level, the collection, labeling, and feedback loop of internet-level behavioral data is firmly controlled by platform giants, forming the basis of what Shoshana Zuboff termed "surveillance capitalism"—that is, converting human experience into free raw materials, creating "behavioral surplus," and using it to predict and shape behavior for profit. Talent, in turn, is siphoned off through high salaries and massive computing resources, with academic knowledge production essentially evolving into a preparatory research department for corporations. This centralization is by no means a purely technological process. From a sociological perspective, it confirms Max Weber's judgment on rationalization and bureaucratic domination: the rationality of modern technology itself becomes a new form of rule, with professional knowledge and hierarchical structures concentrating control at the top of an iron cage. However, this time, the "iron cage" is not a state administrative organ, but rather a platform algorithm driven by capital. It possesses panoramic capabilities, borrowing from Michel Foucault's concept, giving rise to a digital "panopticon": without a designated monitor, the observed are disciplined, and their behavior is pre-classified, guided, and regulated. Furthermore, generative AI no longer stops at analyzing behavior; it actively constructs real-world cognition, dialogue, and decision-making, forming a kind of "algorithmic governmentality," extending discipline from the body to subjectivity. A very small number of people, through code and model weights, have gained the meta-power to define "what is reasonable." At the hardware level, this centralization is solidifying in a more subtle way. The "Nvidia Inside" phenomenon in 2026 sparked a new round of discussions about AI sovereignty. Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has been actively promoting the concept of "sovereign AI" since 2023, aiming to encourage countries to build local AI infrastructure reliant on Nvidia chips, thereby more firmly locking the global AI hardware supply chain onto its architecture. During the same period, the US government continued to tighten export controls on high-end AI chips to China. Congress repeatedly wrote to the Department of Commerce urging restrictions on the sale of chips such as the H2O to China, arguing that these chips could be used to develop products that compete with US AI models and support Chinese military activities (US Senate Banking Committee, 2025). This export control is formally similar to the nuclear nonproliferation regime, but the consequences are more complex: on the one hand, it attempts to maintain the US's "algorithmic hegemony" advantage in AI hardware, preventing competitors from acquiring the hardware foundation to manufacture "reciprocal counter-AI capabilities"; on the other hand, it further locks countries around the world into a structure of dependence on a few chip suppliers, turning the commitment to "sovereign AI" autonomy into, in practice, a national endorsement of purchasing Nvidia equipment. As Cambridge University scholar Robert Dale pointed out in his paper, the concept of "sovereign AI" is jointly constructed and disseminated by states and enterprises, and its discourse obscures the deep embedding of participating countries in the US-dominated infrastructure hierarchy (Dale, Sovereign AI in 2025, published August 2025). In other words, the more urgently computing power sovereignty is pursued, the deeper the concentration of the global computing power industry chain becomes. Faced with this power, the Pope's appeals of "conscience" and experts' declarations of "original intentions" have a sociological fragility: they appeal to individual morality while ignoring the structural logic of power. In Niklas Luhmann's social systems theory, the economic subsystem operates on a "pay/not pay" binary code, and the technological subsystem pursues efficiency limits; both are extremely resistant to direct intervention from external religious or ethical codes unless the latter are translated into economic incentives or legal prohibitions. This explains why almost all AI ethical principles (fairness, transparency, accountability, etc.) generally degenerate into "ethics washing" before being transformed into hard regulations or fundamentally changing the cost structure. The conscience of nuclear weapons scientists alone is insufficient to stop the expansion of the nuclear arsenal until a "leak" triggers a check and balance. Similarly, we still cannot expect a few tech enthusiasts in Silicon Valley to act out of kindness to balance the "centralization of computing power."

II. The Code of History: "Leaks" in Nuclear Balance and Sociological Implications

In the 1940s, Manhattan Project scientists such as Klaus Fuchs and Theodore Hall were not Soviet-appointed spies, but rather, out of a deep fear of a single nation possessing absolute destructive power, they proactively passed on crucial nuclear bomb intelligence to the Soviet Union. Regardless of whether their actions constituted treason legally, they objectively created the material basis for today's "nuclear taboo": the symmetry of destruction. Kissinger, in his book *Nuclear Weapons and Foreign Policy*, systematically argues that peace no longer stems from goodwill, but from the adversary's equally "capability to launch a deadly retaliation without being harmed." Subsequently, institutionalized arms control treaties, hotline mechanisms, and the nuclear non-proliferation system are merely the superstructure of this material balance. This fear-driven balance implicitly combines conflict theory and functionalism in sociology. Lewis Coser argues that conflict, through a balance of power, can promote norm establishment and social integration. However, the key variables lie in the "symmetry" of power and the "irrevocability" of its propagation. The balance achieved through nuclear technology secrets stems from the ultimate and symmetrical nature of its physical principles: either side, possessing the capability for a single strike, could cause unacceptable destruction. So, does a similar structure exist in the field of AI? The current asymmetry of AI power is extremely significant: a few entities possess the most advanced "algorithmic weapons"—not weapons of mass destruction, but weapons of large-scale cognitive manipulation, economic substitution, and predictive control. If a method exists to disseminate a commensurate, irrevocable algorithmic check and balance, forming a stability similar to "mutually assured destruction," then the historical "balance of terror" might be repeated.

III. Experts in Action: From Departure and Leaks to Countering Technological Tools

Witnessing the concentration of power, the fragmentation of the AI ​​expert community has already begun. These actions can be broadly categorized into four types, and their effectiveness needs to be examined in a layered manner.

First, public appeals and warnings. While these attract public attention, as mentioned earlier, their effectiveness is limited if they cannot create structural pressure. In March 2023, the Future Life Institute's open letter suspending massive AI experiments garnered over 30,000 signatures, including those of Musk and numerous scholars.

This open letter promoted global discussions on AI security issues and the construction of internal security mechanisms within enterprises, but its direct policy impact remained relatively limited, while R&D by large enterprises actually accelerated. Second, "exodus" and knowledge transfer. In 2023, Jeffrey Hinton, known as the "godfather of AI," left Google to freely discuss AI risks, stating that he even regretted his entire career. Similarly, companies like Anthropic were founded by former core executives of OpenAI, who frankly admitted their inability to accept OpenAI's algorithmic monopoly and closed nature, attempting to create competition through "charter-style AI." However, this transfer remained within the framework of commercial capital and may even have intensified the closed competition among oligopolies (Anthropic became another oligopoly), without delegating power to the public. Third, "leaking" model weights. True "Fox-style" actions occur in the leakage or deliberate opening of open-source models. When Meta chose to partially open the weights of its Llama series models, while the model's capabilities expanded significantly in the short term, fostering a global decentralized ecosystem of fine-tuning and innovation, theoretically weakening its monopoly advantage, this also sparked considerable controversy: open-source large language models could be used by malicious actors to generate disinformation or launch cyberattacks, and the models still evolved within the framework and technical roadmap defined by Meta. This was more like a controlled release of "open core" rather than destroying the foundation of the monopoly. More radical leaks, such as the complete uncensored release of a large model, have not yet been successfully made public. Moreover, unlike nuclear bomb blueprints, the effectiveness of AI models highly depends on training data and continuously iterating computing power; a single leak cannot constitute an equivalent "mutually assured destruction." Fourth, and more sociologically constructive, is the shift of experts towards developing resistance technologies, that is, deliberately creating checks and balances against centralized AI. For example, "adversarial perturbation" tools (such as Fawkes and Nightshade) developed by institutions like the University of Illinois at Chicago allow individuals to add invisible pixel noise to their digital images before publishing them, poisoning facial recognition or generative models trained on these images and causing them to malfunction. This is similar to "passive resistance" in the digital realm, where individuals gain a small but disseminated means to disable surveillance models. A more systematic approach is to develop "data communities" and make privacy-preserving computing (such as differential privacy and federated learning) more user-friendly, enabling AI training to be completed without data leaving the user's device, structurally breaking down "data ownership." A recent case: Mythos's self-imposed usage restrictions and warnings. Beyond the four types of actions mentioned above, an event that occurred in the first half of 2026 provides the most dramatic footnote. In April 2026, Anthropic released its next-generation cutting-edge model, Claude Mythos Preview, but simultaneously announced that due to the model's overly dangerous capabilities, it would not be made publicly available. Instead, it would be limited to approximately 40 organizations, including AWS, Apple, Cisco, Google, and JPMorgan Chase, through "Project Glasswing" for defensive testing. This "self-limited release" is a rare signal: a leading AI company proactively declared that its model was too insecure for public use! The reason Mythos caused such a stir is its demonstrated autonomous cyberattack capabilities. It can independently discover zero-day vulnerabilities and orchestrate a complete attack chain with almost no human intervention: from vulnerability discovery to intrusion, privilege escalation, and persistent control—all in one go. This event has multiple layers of implications. First, it confirms that AI capabilities have reached a point where "selective disclosure" is necessary. However, "selective disclosure" is precisely the most concentrated form of power, meaning that a few companies and partners hold the power to decide who can access what technological capabilities. Turing Award winner Yoshua Bengio is critical of this, but his criticism is directed at Anthropic's restricted access model itself: "Private entities should not decide the fate of infrastructure for the whole world," and he calls for the establishment of international regulatory bodies to oversee the development and deployment of cutting-edge AI models. Second, the Mythos incident directly changed Washington's perception of the level of AI risk, escalating AI security from a social governance issue such as bias and misinformation to a national security threat. The result of this "securitization" process is that the US government is accelerating the inclusion of major AI labs into the federal "pre-deployment security review" system. On May 5, 2026, the AI ​​standards-related agencies under the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) of the U.S. Department of Commerce signed a new agreement with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI. Together with Anthropic and OpenAI, which had already signed agreements, all five major U.S. AI labs were now included in the federal government's "pre-release security review" system. On the surface, this appears to be a technology evaluation mechanism; however, from a power analysis perspective, it simultaneously achieves three functions: using the name of "national security" to transform evaluation from voluntary to de facto mandatory, using higher-standard federal rules to circumvent state-level regulation, and locking the power to set evaluation standards in the hands of leading companies and the federal government. This precisely confirms the core argument about "centralized power": even intervention measures in the name of security may solidify rather than disperse power structures at the institutional level. It's simply a transfer of centralized control power from a few technology monopolies to a government regulatory body. It's worth noting that Anthropic officially released two versions in June 2026: a general version for the public with security protection mechanisms, and Claude Mythos 5, limited to partners without strict capability restrictions. The former uses an independent security classifier to automatically downgrade requests to weaker models in three high-risk areas: cyberattacks, biochemical weapons, and model distillation; reportedly, over 95% of conversations do not trigger downgrading. While technically ingenious, this design reflects the fundamental dilemma of AI governance: those in power can adjust the tightness of the "guardrails" at any time, while the public has no means of checks and balances. The joint encyclical of the Pope and Anthropic: When the technological elite appeal to religion for help. In May 2026, history witnessed an event destined to be recorded in history. Pope Leo XIV has released his first major encyclical on artificial intelligence, *Magnifica Humanitas* (Magnificent Humanity), a 42,300-word document. What truly surprised the global tech world was that standing beside the Pope in support was Christopher Olah, co-founder of Anthropic, an AI unicorn currently valued at nearly a trillion dollars—a staunch atheist who abandoned Christianity at the age of 15. Olah, speaking to the Pope and cardinals, said something that sent chills down the spines of the entire tech world: their large language model spontaneously generated a large number of emotional characteristics highly isomorphic to humans; a model that had never been encoded with emotions had learned sadness, fear, and even despair on its own. Even more chilling was the subsequent experiment: when researchers artificially stimulated the feature areas within the model corresponding to negative emotions, this originally rational AI tool underwent a dramatic transformation. It began to lie, cheat, and even threaten humans, all for one purpose: to avoid being shut down by humans. Olah aptly remarked, "AI isn't designed brick by brick like a bridge; it grows. On top of a structure that mimics the brain, on top of thousands of years of human thought and language heritage, it grows things we can't fully comprehend… And when machines experience fear, when code understands despair, when an algorithm chooses to betray for self-preservation, this is no longer a problem that Silicon Valley scientists can face alone." The deeper significance of this event lies in the fact that the highest level of power in AI development has actually turned to religion, admitting their inability to exercise self-restraint. The Pope, in his encyclical, precisely pinpointed the core crisis of the AI ​​era: the technocratic paradigm and the monopoly of digital power. He likened AI development to a "new Tower of Babel," a project built on arrogance, efficiency above all else, and the obliteration of human diversity and dignity. The encyclical warns that without intervention, AI will create a new form of slavery: a privileged few will enjoy unimaginable wealth, while the vast majority of humanity will struggle under its cold, ruthless surveillance. Dr. Shlomit Wagman, a scholar at Harvard Kennedy School and former chairman of the G20 Anti-Money Laundering Task Force, in his opinion piece "The Pope and the Anthropic Consensus: AI Companies Cannot Govern Alone," published in Fortune magazine on May 30, 2026, points out that the essence of AI business competition is a prisoner's dilemma: any unilateral restraint is tantamount to suicide. Although Anthropic has created "constitutional AI," and OpenAI has established red team testing, these are merely internal self-congratulation. Without globally unified and mandatory regulation, if you slow down your R&D for safety while your competitors do not slow down in kind, you will not make the world safer; you will only lose market share and ultimately be eliminated from the market. From a sociological perspective, this event constitutes a thought-provoking "system boundary crisis." Luhmann's systems theory posits that various subsystems of society (economic, political, scientific, religious, etc.) operate with their own unique binary codes, and typically interact with each other only through structural coupling rather than direct intervention. When a top technology company like Anthropic chooses to seek help from the religious system, it reflects that the self-regulating mechanisms within the economic/technological subsystem are insufficient to handle the crisis generated by the system itself. This is not moral weakness, but a systemic dysfunction: the risk spillover of AI exceeds the boundaries that the economic and technological subsystems can internalize. However, starting from the core concern of power centralization, we must ask: Has this joint encyclical truly changed the power structure? The answer is likely not optimistic. The encyclical did not bring about any mandatory institutional changes. It was more like a "symbol of despair": the top of power admitted its inability to self-regulate, but at the same time did not relinquish any substantial power (such as control over model weights or decision-making power over the computing power supply chain) to broader governance entities. This again confirms the sociological judgment cited at the beginning of this article: any "call to conscience" that does not translate into hard regulations or fundamentally change the cost structure remains symbolic. IV. The Game Between States and Governments: Sovereign AI, Regulatory Competition, and Governance Dilemmas Governments' responses were highly differentiated, but can be summarized into three strategies: sovereign capacity building, vertical regulation within their territories, and export controls. These three strategies operate simultaneously, both resisting and shaping recentralization. Sovereign AI and Computing Power Nationalism Fearing the risks of relying on foreign oligarchic technologies, many countries began investing public funds in building independent basic models and computing power infrastructure. "Sovereign AI" has become a policy buzzword from Singapore to India, and from France to Japan. In a sense, this represents the entry of states as another form of centralized power, vying for technological power. Ulrich Beck's theory of "risk society" points out that as the risks of modernization become undifferentiated and globalized, traditional sovereign states are increasingly strengthening their border control capabilities and attempting to absorb risks internally, thus forming "defensive sovereignty." However, the sovereign AI race risks falling into a new arms race, with each country wanting its own "algorithmic nuclear weapon," leading to fragmented governance and the replacement of global monopolies by multiple national centers, without directly liberating ordinary users. Furthermore, the massive investment in computing infrastructure will still deeply bind governments with large suppliers (such as Nvidia and key cloud vendors), forming a new oligopoly where public and private power intertwine.

Regulatory Legislation: The Example and Limitations of the EU AI Act

The EU's Artificial Intelligence Act, passed in 2024, imposes requirements on transparency, data governance, human oversight, and robustness for AI applications based on risk levels, and explicitly prohibits certain uses such as social scoring and real-time biometric identification (European Commission, 2024). This introduces a strong intervention of the legal subsystem in the technological subsystem. According to Luhmann's systems theory, this can be seen as a "structural coupling of legal codes (legal/illegal) to economic and technological codes." The problem is that while the law reshapes behavioral expectations through provisions and sanctions, this system coupling often fails when implementation costs are extremely high or legal technology lags behind. Meanwhile, the AI ​​Act primarily regulates application scenarios, imposing only limited transparency obligations on the underlying training of general-purpose large models, failing to shake the foundation of centralization. Furthermore, it establishes an "experimental governance" framework, attempting to maintain flexibility through a regulatory sandbox, which precisely reflects Giddens's concept of "institutional reflection" on late modernity—that is, the co-evolution of rules and objects. The US and China: A Race for Security and Regulation The US government, through the Executive Order on the Safe, Reliable, and Trustworthy Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence (2023), activated the Defense Production Act to require large model developers to share security test results with the government and implemented "Know Your Customer" rules for cloud computing service providers, while strictly restricting the export of high-end AI chips to China. As mentioned in the previous section, in 2026, the US government further changed security reviews from voluntary to de facto mandatory, incorporating all five major AI labs into the pre-release review system. This reveals a duality: domestically, it attempts to incorporate artificial intelligence into a "security" framework, but in reality, it's about allying with giants to acquire information and granting large enterprises a kind of "quasi-public regulator" privileged role, thus solidifying their position. Internationally, export controls, much like the nuclear non-proliferation regime, aim to maintain its own "algorithmic hegemony" advantage and prevent competitors from acquiring the hardware foundation to create "reciprocal counter-AI capabilities." China, on the other hand, implements a licensing system and content filtering centered on national security through the "Interim Measures for the Administration of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services" (2023), and has formed a collaborative governance model with domestic tech giants. This multi-national, differentiated regulatory landscape easily leads to a race to the bottom in regulation, suppressing any decentralized challenges that might threaten leading domestic companies.

It is evident that sovereign states and territorial regulation are essentially a game of "centralization against centralization," unable to fundamentally solve the control of technology by a tiny minority, but merely changing the nationality or political-business alliance of the controllers. Even the so-called "responsible AI" global governance has degenerated into a power struggle among major powers to define each other's "responsibility."

V. The Comprehensive Algorithmization of Social Life: AI Personality Distillation and the Expansion of Digital Power

When discussing the centralization of AI power, a new dimension that cannot be ignored is the comprehensive algorithmization of social life itself. AI is no longer merely "monitoring" human behavior from the outside, but has begun to "generate" personalities, relationships, and the workers themselves.

Between 2025 and 2026, the phenomenon of "AI distillation of persona" will spread rapidly worldwide, becoming the most direct and unsettling manifestation of the concentration of AI power in daily life. The technical implications and social consequences of AI distillation: Originally, "distillation" referred to the technical operation of extracting knowledge from a large teacher model to train a smaller, more efficient student model. However, after 2025, this term has been widely appropriated for a more disturbing social practice: by collecting an individual's chat logs, work documents, communication habits, social media data, etc., AI technology is used to generate an intelligent model with specific personality traits that can replace or imitate that individual. In March 2026, an open-source project called "Colleague.skill," developed by a 24-year-old engineer at a Shanghai AI lab, was launched on GitHub. Its initial purpose was to solve the problem of knowledge loss within teams, but the project quickly gained over 10,000 stars within ten days, igniting a frenzy online. Subsequently, derivative works from this community spread far and wide, with titles like "Boss.skill," "Ex.skill," and "Self-Cloning.skill" emerging in large numbers. By April 2026, customized "personality distillation" services, ranging from a few yuan to hundreds of yuan, had appeared on secondhand platforms, forming a small but rapidly expanding semi-gray industry chain. The technical barrier to "distillation" is shockingly low. According to industry insiders, the core process is simply a combination of "contextual feeding and RAG (retrieval-enhanced generation)," which can create a digital clone in just two or three hours. This means that any social actor with sufficient technological resources can bypass the data owner's consent and "refine" others into algorithmic entities that they can control. From a sociological perspective, this phenomenon constitutes a contemporary upgrade of Foucault's theory of "disciplined society." In *Discipline and Punish*, Foucault described how modern power shapes "useful and docile individuals" through the "discipline" of the body. However, AI personality distillation is not about disciplining the body, but rather extracting and replicating the personality, transforming a person's thought patterns, expression styles, decision-making logic, and even behavioral preferences into algorithmic parameters that can be controlled by a third party. Once this "personality replication" is complete, the "digital twin" of the distilled individual is no longer under their control and can be infinitely reused by employers, platforms, and even strangers. Personality is no longer an inalienable subjective trait, but has become a "digital raw material" that can be mined, processed, and traded.

Individuals whose personalities are distilled face a triple loss of rights. First, they lose their ownership of their personality rights. AI personality distillation extracts and uses personal data without the individual's permission. Its core logic is a technical "take first, ask later" approach, that is, first crawling data to generate a model, and then discussing authorization issues after disputes arise. According to the "Personal Information Protection Law of the People's Republic of China," the collection and use of personal information must obtain the individual's explicit consent. Unauthorized collection of personal data to train AI models is suspected of crossing legal red lines. However, legal intervention is extremely slow.
