Even if an individual discovers that their "AI clone" is circulating online, tracing the data source, securing evidence, and identifying the infringer is almost impossible in the face of the "algorithm black box." Courts have also ruled in actual cases that even if a company owns the copyright to works such as recordings, using the personality characteristics of others for AI distillation without permission still constitutes an infringement of personality rights. However, when the burden of proof falls on the rights defender, while the technology provider controls all the core evidence, legal remedies are often difficult to enforce. Secondly, there is the power restructuring in labor relations. One of the most disturbing applications of AI personality distillation is replacing real workers with "digital clones" generated through distillation. According to media reports, some companies have used "colleague.skill" to import data from former employees' Lark, DingTalk, and emails, training AI clones to continue handling tasks such as HR consultations, weekly reports, and code reviews. By using this tool, companies can continue to "freely" use employees' knowledge and methodologies after they leave. Employees' personal experience, external learning outcomes, and non-job-related inventions are "cloned" without authorization, constituting an infringement on employees' intellectual property. From a power analysis perspective, this means that workers are not only subject to "panoramic" monitoring by algorithms during their work period, but their labor achievements and personality traits remain locked in the company's algorithm system after leaving, becoming a digital labor asset that can be infinitely replicated and never "retires." The relationship between workers and their products of labor is completely severed, and AI distillation technology provides an extremely efficient tool for this severance. Legal experts have explicitly pointed out that the application of AI distillation technology in labor relations crosses multiple legal red lines: it may not only constitute an infringement of personality rights and privacy rights, but at the labor relations level, it may also involve illegally terminating labor relations under the pretext of AI intelligent upgrades, and indirectly coercing employees to sign data authorization documents. However, the existing legal system is clearly lagging behind in dealing with this complex chain of "AI distillation—personality substitution—labor substitution." Once AI-generated content infringes on rights, makes errors, or leaks information, existing laws cannot clearly define the responsible party, ultimately creating a legal vacuum where "everyone is responsible, but no one is accountable." Thirdly, the erosion of subjectivity and the commodification of people. At a deeper level, AI personality distillation is a commodification of "people" themselves. When a person's knowledge, style, and even personality are arbitrarily dismantled, digitized, and labeled, the person quietly transforms from a subject of value into a tool, from a complete individual into "digital raw materials" that can be mined, processed, and traded. This process suggests a more somber aspect of the centralization of AI power: a tiny minority of controllers not only monitor and shape human behavior through AI, but also use AI personality distillation technology to transform human existence itself into extractable, reusable, and tradable algorithmic assets. In Foucault's words, this is no longer a matter of "discipline," but a fundamental appropriation of "subjectivity." The distilled individual doesn't even need to be monitored; their personality has already been copied and operates independently, completely decoupled from their own will and interests. From the perspective of data power, AI personality distillation is an extension and upgrade of Zuboff's theory of "surveillance capitalism." The core mechanism of surveillance capitalism is the transformation of human experience into "behavioral surplus" for predicting behavior. AI distillation, however, goes beyond predicting behavior; it creates a copy of the personality that can "act independently"—an algorithmic worker that never stops, never tires, and never resigns. This behavioral surplus no longer remains at the analytical level but is directly input into the production process, fundamentally altering the nature of labor relations. From the perspective of Luhmann's systems theory, the economic subsystem here successfully translates personality traits into its internal pay/non-pay codes: after paying a one-time distillation cost, one obtains an "infinitely usable personality capital." However, the cost of this translation is the systematic marginalization of the intervention capabilities of the legal subsystem (personality rights, labor rights, privacy rights) and the political subsystem (civil rights), because no one can exercise sovereignty over these algorithmically replicated copies of their own personalities. The rise of AI personality distillation has transformed our core concern from a macro-level structural issue into a concrete threat that everyone may encounter in their daily lives. This threat is possible precisely because of the high concentration of computing power, models, and data: only entities that control large-scale computing power and model training capabilities can implement personality distillation at such a broad and deep level. In this context, the decentralized data sovereignty and identity management advocated by blockchain technology have gained new urgency. VI. Soft Constraints of International Law and the Gray Rhino of Quantum Computing Soft Constraints of International Law and the Defects of the “Global Digital Charter” At the international legal level, a series of declarations and initiatives have emerged, such as the UNESCO Recommendation on Ethical Issues of Artificial Intelligence adopted in 2021, and the UN General Assembly resolution adopted for the first time in 2024, “Seizing the Opportunities of Safe, Reliable and Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence Systems” (UN, 2024). These documents advocate for human-centeredness, inclusiveness, fairness, and non-discrimination. Unfortunately, they are all soft laws, lacking enforcement mechanisms and relying on reputational pressure and internalization of norms. In the sociological field of international relations, for such norms to create a "norm cascade" and ultimately become internalized into state behavior, strong norm advocates and incentives are needed. AI norms, however, face fundamental disagreements: technologically leading countries view them as non-binding guidelines, technologically catching-up countries see them as bargaining chips for obtaining technological assistance, and civil society is often marginalized by the technological optimism captured by capital. To date, the only truly prohibitive international initiative concerning power structures is the framework of the Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons, which discusses "killer robots," but no convention has yet been formed to address the larger-scale, everyday monopoly of computing power. Expecting international law to limit the concentration of AI power in the foreseeable future is tantamount to waiting for Godot. The Gray Rhino of Quantum Computing: The Collapse of Encryption Systems and the Recentralization of Power The main source of risk for the centralization of AI power lies in the AI ​​models and algorithms themselves. However, a rapidly approaching "gray rhino" event—the systemic threat of quantum computing to existing encryption systems—is creating a completely new risk of power recentralization. This topic is particularly important for understanding whether blockchain can rein in AI, because the security of blockchain is built upon existing encryption systems. Quantum computing, with its exponentially superior computing power compared to classical computers for specific computational tasks, poses a fundamental threat to widely used asymmetric encryption algorithms such as RSA and ECC. A bipartisan congressional advisory committee explicitly warned at a hearing in December 2025: “AI can launch faster and more dangerous cyberattacks, while quantum computers can break current encryption standards and expose sensitive data. These capabilities will be weaponized by our adversaries, creating an extremely dangerous imbalance in cyber defense.” (U.S. House Homeland Security Committee, 2025) The threat of quantum computing is particularly troubling on a timescale. Industry experts widely predict that the critical inflection point of “quantum advantage” is only three to five years away. Marco Pistoia, Senior Vice President of Global Industry Relations at IonQ, warned even more bluntly: “An attack on a country could very well be a quantum attack, breaking sensitive communications of governments, banks, or medical institutions.” Currently, the global cybersecurity field is widely warning of “harvest now, decrypt later” attack strategies: currently encrypted data is being collected in large quantities, which can be completely decrypted all at once once quantum computers mature. The combination of artificial intelligence and quantum computing may usher in a new level of technological progress. AI accelerates quantum development, while quantum computing provides AI with unprecedented computing power. The emergence of quantum computing will create a triple crisis. First, the fundamental security of blockchain faces disruption. Currently, the security of all mainstream blockchains relies on asymmetric encryption algorithms such as ECC. Once quantum computers reach sufficient power, private keys can be reverse-engineered, and the trust foundation of blockchain will be uprooted. This means that if blockchain is to become a tool against AI monopolies, it must complete post-quantum cryptography (PQC) upgrades before quantum computing destroys its security foundation. The standardization, deployment, and global coordination of PQC is itself a huge governance challenge, requiring synchronized upgrades globally. Any lagging node could become an entry point for attacks. As of 2026, although the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) has completed the standardization of the first batch of PQC algorithms, large-scale global deployment is still in its early stages, and the overall transition window is quite tight. Secondly, encrypted social spaces will be re-exposed to the gaze of power. Encryption technology is currently the core defense against mass surveillance, whether it's end-to-end encrypted messaging applications or anonymous cryptocurrency transactions, all are built on existing encryption systems. The emergence of quantum computers will systematically destroy this defense, making the power asymmetry at the data level reach an unprecedented level. Countries or companies that possess quantum capabilities will be able to access all encrypted data, from personal private communications to financial institution transaction records, from medical records to government secrets. In Foucault's terms, this is a double reinforcement of the digital "panopticon": not only does AI construct a control structure from the production end, but quantum computing dismantles the last bastion of privacy from the defense end. Thirdly, the "winner-takes-all" structure of quantum technology itself will exacerbate centralization. Quantum computing differs from AI, which can still undergo a certain degree of decentralized fine-tuning and reasoning with decreasing marginal costs, while the hardware threshold for quantum computing is extremely high. Superconducting quantum bit systems require operation under extreme conditions close to absolute zero, making their manufacturing and maintenance extremely expensive. This means that the ownership of quantum capabilities will be concentrated in a very small number of financially powerful and scientifically advanced countries and super-corporations, making it virtually impossible for ordinary companies, organizations, and individuals to access or counter this capability. Once quantum computing matures, its owners will possess the ability to "break all encryption," which will translate into absolute dominance over the digital world. As pointed out in the US Congressional hearings, "future attacks on a nation are likely to be quantum attacks," but more profoundly, quantum capabilities themselves will become a kind of "meta-power," capable of unlocking everything protected by encryption, thus fundamentally undermining the function of encryption as a tool for checks and balances. From the perspective of Coser's conflict theory, the emergence of quantum computing may not be creating a new power equilibrium, but rather a new power imbalance. In the nuclear age, the US and the Soviet Union successively acquired nuclear capabilities, creating a "balance of terror." However, the technological threshold for quantum computing is far higher than that of nuclear weapons. The number of countries or companies capable of building and deploying stable, error-correcting quantum computers on a large scale is likely far fewer than that of nuclear-armed states. Even more worrying is the synergistic effect of quantum computing and AI: AI can help quantum systems correct errors and optimize more efficiently, while quantum computing can provide AI with the means to crack any encryption system, thereby helping a very small number of controllers obtain unlimited training data and monitoring capabilities. Once this "quantum-AI" dual monopoly is formed, its power concentration will far exceed the existing AI oligopoly, exposing all information flows in human society to its gaze. This "gray rhino" presents a fundamental challenge: given that encryption security will be destroyed by quantum computing, the promises of blockchain such as "immutability" and "no need for third parties" will face fundamental erosion. If blockchain cannot complete its quantum-resistant upgrade before the large-scale deployment of quantum computing, it will not even be able to protect itself, let alone counterbalance the AI ​​monopoly. Judging from the current progress of international coordination and deployment, the window of opportunity for PQC transition is likely to be tighter than expected. The United Nations has declared 2025 the "International Year of Quantum Science and Technology," which in itself represents a global consensus on the transformative potential of quantum technology. However, consensus does not equate to action. VII. The Sociological Imagination of Decentralization: Can Blockchain Play the Role of a "Leaker"? This is our core consideration: Since expert resistance, state power struggles, international law, and the quantum gray rhino all have fundamental flaws, can the "decentralized" technological paradigm, as the core of Web3.0, be translated into a structural check and balance against AI monopolies? The original promise of blockchain is to create a peer-to-peer network that does not require trust in third parties, achieving decentralized control through consensus mechanisms. Mapping this logic to the AI ​​field, it can theoretically be broken down into three levels: decentralized computing power, decentralized data, and decentralized model governance. We need to examine the true relationship between these levels and the checks and balances on AI power one by one. Decentralized Computing Power and Data Alliances: Challenge or Dependence? Currently, there are blockchain-based markets, such as Akash Network and iExec RLC, attempting to establish decentralized cloud computing resource markets, allowing any individual or institution with idle GPUs to rent out computing power and receive token rewards. Similarly, Ocean Protocol and Fetch.ai attempt to incentivize data sharing and federated learning with token economics, returning data rights to the providers. This attempts to redistribute ownership of "means of production" (computing power and data), challenging the monopoly of large cloud computing platforms. From a sociological perspective, such schemes borrow from Elinor Ostrom's (2009 Nobel laureate in economics) principles of self-organized governance of common-pool resources: clearly defined boundaries, rules of use and provision commensurate with local conditions, collective choice arrangements, oversight, and tiered sanctions (Governance of Commons: The Evolution of Institutions of Collective Action, 1990). If AI resources are viewed as a "common pond" in the digital age, then community-driven market and governance mechanisms may be able to avoid the tragedy of the commons and oligopolistic encroachment. However, Ostrom's success was based on conditions including a relatively small scale, stable membership, and effective communication, which are difficult to meet in a completely open and anonymous global blockchain network. More importantly, governance within blockchain networks can also be infiltrated by power: under proof-of-stake mechanisms, computing power providers and large token holders can easily form new concentrations. In fact, large-scale AI decentralized networks (such as Bittensor) have already seen a situation where a few large nodes control most of the model output and revenue, tending towards Robert Michels' "ironclad law of oligopoly": any organization, even if it starts democratically, will evolve into an oligopoly. Despite this, against the backdrop of deepening AI power centralization, a new, more politically conscious consensus is emerging within the blockchain community. Decentralized AI projects that combat monopolistic, centralized AI are gaining wider sympathy and support. A commentator who has long observed the intersection of blockchain and AI wrote, "I now tend to think that any decentralized AI project that combats monopolistic, centralized AI is worth watching, because the more forces that fight against that monopoly, the less likely future disasters will occur." Looking ahead to the integration of blockchain and AI, he pointed out, "Cryptocurrencies will begin to wrest power from large tech companies and return it to users." While such voices have a technological optimism tinged with optimism, they reflect the blockchain field's serious consideration of the issue of "decentralization as resistance." Decentralized Models and Censorship-Resistant Algorithms: Can "Algorithmic Leaks" Be Achieved? An even more disruptive idea is to create artificial intelligence that cannot be shut down by any single entity, running on a decentralized virtual machine. For example, by storing model parameters and inference processes on decentralized storage like IPFS or Arweave, and having smart contracts or decentralized autonomous organizations (DAOs) control API access and revenue distribution, this sounds like the institutionalization of "algorithmic leaks": once the model weights are public and operating in this way, no state censorship or corporate extraction can make them disappear, thus creating a "non-monopolizable foundational AI capability." Some zero-knowledge proof machine learning (ZKML) projects attempt to prove the correctness of inference while hiding the specific inputs, balancing privacy and trust. This is similar in intent to the Fox News leak effect: disseminating capabilities to the point of universal access, thereby eliminating the overwhelming advantage of one side. However, a fundamental "power reversion" paradox exists here: who controls the underlying blockchain itself? If the blockchain remains dominated by a foundation or early-stage capital oligarchs, decentralized AI is merely adding a new layer of dependence on top of the existing "miners." Furthermore, the scale of capital and energy required to train the basic model makes it extremely difficult for the decentralized community to create a foundational model from scratch that can rival OpenAI or Google. More likely, they will fork or fine-tune existing open-source models, which still implicitly remains dependent on the original creators' path and power. The current AI technology stack, from hardware instruction sets to frameworks to model architectures, is almost entirely defined in a closed loop by large companies and related groups. The blockchain community attempts to reopen this "closed loop," but is marginalized due to a lack of core resources defining the technical problems. This is likely to create a pattern of "marginal innovation dependence," making it difficult to overturn the centralized order. The Utopia and Dilemma of Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) DAOs are envisioned as replacing human governance with code governance, managing AI infrastructure through collective voting. However, the deep governance experiments of existing DAOs are not optimistic. Anthropological observations show that many DAOs (Democratic Action Organizations) fail due to voter apathy, manipulation by whales (large investors), and internal factions, ultimately resulting in a small number of technical execution teams or capital holders gaining real decision-making power. Michels's ironclad rule has been proven true again, even amplified by the incentives of the crypto economy. Giving control of AI to a DAO is likely just replacing shareholder meetings and boards of directors with token oligarchs, with even more ambiguous accountability. If a global AI network without legal personality, operating solely on code, commits wrongdoing, who will be held accountable? The radical libertarian notion of "code is law" can be explained in systems theory as an attempt to completely replace legal codes with the binary codes of technology, but this will quickly collapse in social conflict.

Summary of the Core Contradictions in the Balance of Power between Blockchain and AI

In summary, there are three structural contradictions in the balance of power between blockchain and AI.

First, the contradiction of scale asymmetry: Combating AI monopolies requires massive computing power and high-quality data, but the decentralized network of blockchain cannot compete with centralized ultra-large-scale clusters in terms of efficiency and scale. This means that any decentralized AI project can only operate on the "edge of the technology stack defined by giants." Second, the paradox of oligopolistic governance: Blockchain attempts to decentralize power through consensus algorithms, but the token economy naturally creates large holders, and technical decisions are highly dependent on the core development team, making "decentralization" often a narrative rather than a fact. Third, the spillover threat of quantum computing: As mentioned earlier, quantum computing will destroy existing encryption systems within the next three to five years, while blockchain's quantum-resistant upgrade is not yet complete. This means that the reliable window of opportunity for blockchain as a check and balance tool is extremely narrow. VIII. The Cracks in Possibilities: A Cautious Outlook Based on Theory At this point, we must admit that hoping that blockchain will completely solve the problem of concentrated AI power is a misplacement of specific technological determinism. Social power structures are far more resilient than technological architectures. But does this mean that decentralized AI completely lacks any value in checks and balances? Sociology suggests that we should look for "constructable antagonism" rather than a one-size-fits-all solution. As a "counter-power" fissure, Foucault's later discourse on resistance emphasizes that power relations are fluid and reversible, and the potential for checks and balances lies in the "resistance of the governed" and "unwillingness to be ruled in this way." Even if decentralized AI tools cannot replace giants, they can provide civil society, investigative journalists, human rights organizations, and others with low-cost analysis and content generation capabilities that are not subject to platform algorithm censorship and blocking, constituting a form of "technological empowerment." For example, decentralized inference networks allow the invocation of open-source models without exposing requests to centralized APIs, which is a circumvention of surveillance capital. When this circumvention reaches a certain scale, it may break the "panopticon of algorithms." It does not require symmetrical checks and balances, but only needs to create enough uncertainty to make the cost of rule unacceptable. Ostrom's experience shows that beyond the public-private dichotomy, multi-layered nested governance can manage complex resources. AI governance can partially adopt a multi-center approach: hard laws such as the EU's Artificial Intelligence Act set the bottom line, national institutions supervise, industry standard organizations formulate mid-level norms such as data quality, and decentralized token ecosystems can be used to incentivize public evaluation and model auditing, such as participating in "red team attacks" by staking tokens to verify model security, forming a semi-decentralized vault. If blockchain itself can be designed with "Rococo-style" checks and balances, that is, its consensus mechanism not only relies on economic rights but also couples reputation, identity, and community contributions, it may be able to mitigate the iron law of oligopoly. However, all of this requires deliberate, non-laissez-faire institutional design, which is inherently in conflict with blockchain fundamentalism. The Metaphor of Historical Counterfactuality: The Triggering Conditions of "Algorithmic Equilibrium" Returning to the analogy of nuclear balance, the ultimate "balance of terror" was achieved not only because of leaks but also because nuclear weapons possess the characteristic of "instantly causing unacceptable destruction." AI monopolies do not have a one-time catastrophic button, but rather a kind of slowly infiltrating control. Therefore, completely replicating nuclear checks and balances is impossible. However, a "distributed equilibrium" in the information domain may exist: if everyone can easily possess a personal AI assistant that cannot be completely banned, whose decisions can simulate and predict manipulative content and issue warnings, then centralized cognitive monopolies will be dissolved. This requires the universalization and inalienability of algorithmic capabilities. Blockchain can serve as the distributed ledger foundation to ensure "inalienability," but the democratization of the underlying large-scale model still requires breakthroughs in hardware and algorithms. The danger lies in the fact that this capability can also be maliciously used, forming a chaotic equilibrium of "mutually assured deception," causing a surge in social trust costs. The Deceptive Trap We Must Be Wary Of: We must always be wary of "decentralization washing": large corporations establish their own "decentralized" consortium blockchains or councils, but in reality, they completely control nodes and governance, misleading the public into believing that power has been decentralized, thus abandoning political action. Anthony Giddens' "disembedding" mechanism here is a variation of symbolic manipulation, where expert systems regain trust by creating symbolic decentralized symbols without changing substantive power. Truly effective decentralized checks and balances can only take root amidst a multi-faceted struggle involving legally mandated transparency, anti-monopoly measures, and public investment by the state in basic computing power. Conclusion: Face the Crisis and Take Action: Faced with the possibility of a tiny minority controlling all of humanity through silicon-based technology, we examined four paths of checks and balances: experts, the state, international law, and blockchain. Expert leaks and countermeasures provided sparks of micro-level resistance, while the Mythos incident and the joint papal encyclical of Anthropic revealed the powerlessness and despair of AI developers themselves. National regulation has become embroiled in a sovereignty race, with the Nvidia Inside phenomenon and the "gray rhino" of quantum computing exacerbating hardware concentration. International law remains a soft declaration. While blockchain, as a decentralized sociological device, demonstrates the potential to shake the monopolies of computing power, data, and models, it is deeply mired in the ironclad rules of oligopolies and the trap of dependency, far from being a cure for monopolies. The phenomenon of AI personality distillation further reveals that the consequences of AI power centralization have permeated everyone's daily life; personality is being digitized, commodified, and capitalized, while countermeasures are severely lagging. The fundamental reason lies in the fact that technology is embedded in the functional differentiation and power asymmetry of modern capitalist society, and no single technological solution can rewrite the social code. However, this does not mean giving up. Historically, every breakthrough in monopolies has not been due to perfect alternatives, but rather to the accumulation of multiple fissures: internal contradictions within the hegemony, the emergence of external competitors, daily resistance from users, and the continuous erosion by alternative practices. The nuclear balance was not achieved through ethical evolution, but rather through the "betrayal" of some to achieve a balance of power. What we need today may not be a ready-made decentralized savior, but rather the conscious creation, at the intersection of technology, law, and social movements, of concrete practices that can decentralize the "right to define algorithms." These practices include supporting non-commercial open-source foundational models, establishing legislation that establishes data as an inalienable personal right, developing public digital infrastructure governed by multiple stakeholders, and maintaining blockchain-style verification rather than control, making the concentration of power vulnerable to challenge and reversal at any time. In sociological terms, this transforms AI from a "closed technology of domination" into an "agonistic arena." Pessimism brings clarity, clarity brings reflection, reflection recognizes the crisis, and the sense of crisis will inspire our action. Because a "balance of terror" requires, first and foremost, the ability to counter terror to be held by multiple stakeholders. In this regard, all of humanity needs to work together to avoid the path to enslavement.

    AI Hot Topics

    1. Morgan Stanley: The Dividend of Artificial Intelligence May Be Benefiting the UK Economy

    On June 12, Morgan Stanley economist Bruna Skarica pointed out in a report that the UK's monthly GDP appears to be benefiting again from the strong performance of white-collar service industries, especially the information and communication technology (ICT) sector. She noted that the sector's output is currently growing by 6.7% year-on-year, and since the fourth quarter of 2019, its output has grown by 45.4%, while the overall economic value added has only grown by 6% during the same period. "The sector most vulnerable to the rapid spread of artificial intelligence is simultaneously driving GDP growth and productivity gains, which seems far from a coincidence," Skarica added. Given that the Bank of England stated last year that the UK's structural productivity growth was negative, the bank should comment more on this this year.

    2. Sochen Technology: Physics AI Business is Still in the Early Stages of Development

    On June 12, Sochen Technology announced that its stock price has fluctuated significantly recently, posing a risk of overheated market sentiment and irrational speculation. The company's stock price has increased by 175.93% cumulatively from April 29, 2026 to June 12, 2026. The company's stock price has significantly deviated from the market index, exhibiting large short-term fluctuations and clearly deviating from market trends, posing a high risk of speculation. The company's recently implemented fundraising project, "Construction of Core Technology Foundation for Physical Synthetic Data and Demonstration Application in Multiple Fields," is still in the preparation stage. If the fundraising investment project fails to be implemented smoothly, the progress of new technology development falls short of expectations, or R&D encounters technical bottlenecks or even fails, it will have a certain impact on the company's operating performance. The company's physics AI business is currently in the early stages of development.

    ... Physical AI technology requires significant R&D investment and undergoes rapid technological iteration, posing risks of slower-than-expected development progress and core technology iteration. Simultaneously, uncertainties exist regarding the expansion of downstream industry application scenarios and commercialization. If market demand, industry policies, and the technological competitive landscape change adversely, the revenue and profit contribution of physical AI business may fall short of expectations.

    3. Epoch AI's Assessment of Mythos: Vulnerability Exploitation 7 Months Ahead, Vulnerability Discovery Advantage Overestimated

    Epoch AI released a research report systematically evaluating the cybersecurity capabilities of Anthropic's Claude Mythos series models. Researchers constructed a "Cyber-ECI" based on multiple security benchmark tests. The results show that Mythos Preview significantly outperforms in vulnerability exploitation development capabilities, advancing approximately 7 months compared to the linear development trend, and also outperforming OpenAI GPT-5.5, which leads the trend by approximately 3 months. Previously, some scholars questioned whether Mythos Preview's performance was comparable to GPT-5.5.

    ... The report points out that the discrepancy stems from the fact that some early assessments used weaker internal versions instead of the April version released to Glasswing partners, and some test sets were already nearing saturation, making it difficult to differentiate between them. Actual security testing shows that Mythos Preview can frequently and autonomously construct arbitrary code execution exploits, something earlier models could hardly do. Regarding vulnerability discovery, there is a lack of strong evidence for a similar leading trend. Although after the release of Mythos Preview, reports of high-risk and critical vulnerabilities (CVEs) from 21 well-known organizations surged by 142% and 262% in April and May, respectively, compared to the 2025 baseline, this surge is likely primarily due to financial investment. Anthropic provided Glasswing with up to $100 million in API credits, attracting a large number of security personnel to conduct concurrent searches in a short period. For example, in the highly scrutinized curl codebase test, Mythos Preview only detected one low-risk vulnerability with four false positives, performing no better than traditional auditing tools. Startup AISLE has also confirmed that some open-source lightweight models can identify the same vulnerabilities. Epoch AI believes that Mythos's actual advantage lies in its extremely low false positive rate and more accurate hazard classification. Compared to directly discovering new vulnerabilities, more accurate classification can help defenders significantly reduce the time and cost of manual review.

    4. Moonshot AI releases and open-sources its latest Kimi K2.7 Code programming model, with a high-speed version launching next Monday

    Moonshot AI has released and open-sourced its latest Kimi K2.7 Code programming model. In long-context programming scenarios, Kimi K2.7 Code improves the performance of instruction compliance and long-running programming tasks, while reducing overthinking tendencies and lowering average token consumption by 30%.

    ... In the Kimi Code Bench v2 benchmark, which evaluates code capabilities, the new model shows a 21.8% performance improvement, and approximately a 10% performance improvement in benchmarks such as Kimi Claw 24/7 Bench, which evaluate the autonomous execution capabilities of intelligent agents. Starting today, enterprises and developers can call Kimi K2.7 Code through the Kimi API open platform.
