data.title: Musk's explosive performance
data.description:
On June 12th, SpaceX, valued at a trillion dollars, is ready to list on Nasdaq—valued at $1.77 trillion and raising $75 billion, potentially making founder Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. Market attention is at an all-time high, and the launch of this "heavy rocket" has drawn the world's attention to this seemingly eccentric man. But what many don't know is that not so long ago, in 2008, the company destroyed three rockets, leaving only enough money for the final launch. Musk himself slept in the factory, raising money to pay employees while being ridiculed by the world's media as the most foolish internet nouveau riche. How did Musk overcome all that challenges along the way? Why was he so determined to build rockets in the first place? What made him so confident that he could accomplish what NASA hadn't been able to do in decades? The newly released book by CITIC Press, *The Musk Principle*, uses Musk's own words for the first time, presenting a complete and authentic picture of Musk. This excerpt is from Chapter Eleven of *The Musk Principle*. Musk recounts his personal journey in founding SpaceX. Space Exploration Fanatic

Affordable electric cars for the masses are an inevitable trend.

Even without me, the situation would be the same.

But making human civilization an interstellar civilization is not something that is destined to happen.

I've always been optimistic, otherwise I wouldn't have made these crazy attempts. I think I'm probably a morbid optimist.

I've always been optimistic, otherwise I wouldn't have made these crazy attempts. I think I'm probably one of those morbid optimists.

My initial motivation for founding SpaceX was to understand why humans haven't yet landed on Mars. After the successful Apollo program's manned lunar landing, the logical next step was to send humans to Mars. Year after year, I checked NASA's website, and they didn't seem to have any plans for this. In 1969, humans successfully landed on the moon. The last time humans landed on the moon was in 1972. Now, half a century later, we haven't returned. If the Apollo program represents the pinnacle of human space exploration, and the moon is the limit of human progress into space, then it's a complete tragedy. The Space Shuttle could only send people into low Earth orbit. After the Space Shuttle was retired, the United States no longer had the capability to send people into space orbit. Humanity has gradually lost its drive to explore space. Does this mean that human civilization has reached its peak and is beginning its decline? If you asked people in 1969 what they thought the world would be like 50 years from now, they would certainly imagine that humanity would have established a lunar base, landed on Mars, and even established a Martian base. They would also expect space hotels to be in orbit, and many other amazing things to be found. If you tell them, "We have a device smaller than a deck of cards that can access information worldwide and communicate instantly with anyone on Earth anytime, anywhere, yet the US can't even send people into space," they'll definitely think you're talking nonsense. They'll say: "You have such advanced technology, yet you've made no progress in space exploration?! How is that possible?!" Since selling PayPal in 2001, I've been pondering this question. SpaceX's initial concept wasn't to create a company, but to explore why humans haven't yet landed on Mars. I thought perhaps it was because humanity had lost its drive to explore, so we had to rekindle that drive. Turns out, I was wrong. Humanity doesn't lack the desire to explore space; it simply believes there's no further path forward. If people believe there's no room for improvement, they won't repeatedly strive for progress. There must be something that inspires us, something that makes us proud to be human. The Apollo program is a prime example. Although only 12 people actually landed on the moon, in a sense, we can say that we all landed on the moon. We indirectly experienced the lunar landing through the astronauts' perspective and participated in this adventure together. No one will question the correctness of the Apollo program or deny its greatness. We need more space exploration programs. Be prepared to lose everything. For truly important things, even with slim odds, take a gamble. When I founded SpaceX and Tesla, I estimated that the success rate of both companies was less than 10%. When faced with various entrepreneurial ideas, I don't judge them based on financial returns. What I seek are pressing problems concerning humanity's current well-being and future prospects. If you were to estimate and rank the risk-adjusted returns of different business opportunities, building rockets and building cars would most likely be at the bottom. They are the most foolish investment choices. Company stock prices have never been my yardstick for measuring my own achievements. Many friends advised me against starting a rocket company because they thought it was crazy. Everyone thought the idea was impractical. Some of them had tried to start rocket companies, but all failed. They also advised me against it. A friend made a video, splicing together many scenes of failed rocket launches and explosions, and insisted I watch it. I said, "I've seen all of that." I think they misunderstood my intentions. When I founded SpaceX, I didn't expect it to succeed; I knew very well that it would most likely fail. They tried to dissuade me: "You'll lose everything." I replied, "Well, I was prepared for the worst anyway, so I don't care!" Of course, I care about the money, but my decision to invest in space exploration wasn't based on the rate of return. I don't think: I can invest in real estate, I can invest in shoe manufacturing, and… wow! Space exploration has the highest rate of return! Making a lot of money wasn't my primary goal. Many times I thought I was going to lose everything. How could anyone start both a car company and a rocket company and expect them both to succeed? I certainly wouldn't. I thought the chances of success for both were very low, less than 10%, maybe only 1%. Frankly, I was right. How to win people's trust and support for a Mars mission? To convince the public, you have to think about what can ignite people's passion. What message do we want to convey? What message will resonate with people? If I were an ordinary person taking an objective stance, what would move me? I think we could use rockets to send a small greenhouse to the surface of Mars, filled with plant seeds and a gel to provide moisture and nutrients for the seeds after landing. This greenhouse mission would realize the first-ever survival of Earth life on another planet and break the record for the farthest distance reached by Earth life. We could take a stunning photo of green plants growing against the red Martian background. That would be a moving photo, igniting public discussion. People are often excited by groundbreaking and groundbreaking achievements. I believe that building greenhouses on Mars could inspire enthusiasm for a manned mission to Mars. I'm willing to take half of what I earn on PayPal—that is, $90 million—and invest it in this project, without expecting any return. It's an important cause worth doing. If spending $90 million can secure more funding for NASA and ultimately lead to a human landing on Mars, that will be enough. Additionally, I'm also exploring whether I can afford to build a spacecraft. I wanted to budget for two missions because if we only had a budget for one mission, a single failure could cripple the company and discourage others from pursuing this career. I could reduce the cost of spacecraft, communication equipment, and small greenhouses, but one cost was insurmountable: launch costs. There weren't many options, and the option based in the U.S. was too expensive. Later, I traveled to Russia three times to try and purchase the largest intercontinental ballistic missile in Russia's nuclear arsenal, but without success. In the process, I realized that what we really needed to do was revolutionize space transportation technology. Without a startup to drive rocket technology development, new technologies won't emerge. Such progress either comes from a startup or it simply won't happen at all. So I founded SpaceX in mid-2002, prepared from the beginning for it to fail. Why did I decide to fund SpaceX myself? After selling PayPal, I started thinking about what to do next, wavering between solar energy, electric vehicles, and aerospace. I felt that the chances of success in aerospace were the lowest, and therefore the least likely to attract other entrepreneurs. Nobody was as crazy as me about space exploration, so I figured I'd better start in aerospace first. My first idea, the greenhouse mission, would almost certainly bankrupt me. In comparison, starting a rocket company has a less than 100% chance of losing all my money. The most likely outcome is that I will lose all my money. But are there any other options? Should we let space exploration stagnate? We must do this, or we will be trapped on Earth forever. I don't recommend first-time entrepreneurs choose the aerospace field; it's more suitable for experienced entrepreneurs with sufficient capital and experience. I firmly believe in this principle: If you're not willing to invest your own money, don't ask investors to. Asking others to invest without investing yourself is wrong in my view. I'd rather lose money myself than let my friends or investors suffer losses. In SpaceX's first three rounds of financing, I didn't even seek investment because investors would first ask: What are some previous success stories in this field? Which cases can this project be compared to? If there are almost no success stories in this field, but a mountain of failures, investors naturally won't be interested. For most venture capitalists, rocket projects are far beyond their comprehension; they simply wouldn't dare touch them. Building rockets is difficult. Before founding SpaceX, I had never worked in the manufacturing industry, let alone building rockets. I had to prove that I was indeed capable of working in manufacturing. How to determine if SpaceX has a chance of success? I wanted to understand the fundamental reason for the high cost of rockets—to look at rockets using first principles. How can the Russians build low-cost rockets? There aren't such big differences in other fields. We don't drive Russian-made cars, fly on Russian-made planes, or use Russian-made kitchen appliances. The US is a highly competitive place; we should be able to build cost-effective launch vehicles. I started studying rocket data extensively, trying to understand why they were so expensive. The Delta II rocket used to cost $60 million; now it's $100 million. That's an astonishing figure! And that's just a relatively small rocket; larger rockets cost between $200 million and $400 million. I was furious, and every time I got angry, I would break the problem down again. I studied NASA's long-term suppliers. I found that relying on suppliers like Boeing and Lockheed would be disastrous. First, one of the problems with these large aerospace companies is their extreme risk aversion. Even with more advanced technology, they insist on using older parts, many developed in the 1960s and still in use today. Everyone is doing everything they can to avoid risk and liability. Second, large aerospace companies have a bad habit of outsourcing everything. Extensive outsourcing is common in many industries, but the aerospace industry takes it to an absurd degree. They outsourced the work to subcontractors, who in turn subcontracted to the next level of subcontractors, and so on, layer upon layer. You had to trace back four or five layers to find the people actually doing the work—the ones cutting metal and manufacturing parts. Each additional layer of outsourcing added to the cost, increasing it fivefold until it became ridiculously expensive. I finally understood why rockets are so expensive. Boeing and Lockheed just wanted to maintain the status quo and continue to reap huge profits. Once success lasts too long, people lose their desire to take risks. With that kind of system, humanity will never reach Mars. I assembled a team of engineers who had participated in the development of all the major launch vehicles of the past 30 years, and I conducted feasibility studies with them. In early 2001, we met every Saturday to discuss and find the best solution that balanced launch costs and reliability, eventually arriving at a basic design. The timing was perfect. Just as this feasibility study was completed, we agreed to eBay's offer to acquire PayPal. So, at the same time that deal was finalized, I moved to Los Angeles, the world's most concentrated area of ​​aerospace talent. Did anyone question you at the time? It seems many people said: Musk is a software engineer, what makes him think he can do hardware? Of course. Many reports from that time can still be found online today. The media always called me an internet kid, saying that my starting a rocket company was wishful thinking. They ridiculed SpaceX relentlessly. When it came down to a desperate gamble, the NASA contract was secured. I thought to myself, if SpaceX fails to reach orbit in three consecutive launches, we deserve to go bankrupt. That was my initial thought. In 2006, our first rocket exploded near the launch site. The second launch also failed. But we made progress each time. In 2008, the Falcon rocket failed for the third consecutive time. I only had a budget for three launches. At that point, I gave all my remaining money to Tesla and SpaceX. If we accelerate the process, this money will be just enough for SpaceX's fourth launch. I called everyone into the meeting room and said: We only have one last chance. Get ready, get back to the island base, and launch the rocket. You only have six weeks. Here is the email I sent to the team: "SpaceX's rocket will successfully reach orbit, demonstrating reliable space transportation capabilities. Everyone must have unwavering faith in this. Personally, I will never give up, never. Thank you for your hard work. Now, on to the fourth launch." If we fail, we will be used as a negative example, discouraging others from attempting space exploration. We must persevere at all costs. I will never give up. Unless I die, or become completely immobile. My money was gone; that time was a close call. If we had failed, we would have become just another rocket startup to die on the road. Fortunately, our fourth launch was finally a success. By the time of the successful launch, my cortisol levels were pathologically high. I felt no joy or celebration. I was under immense pressure. The rocket's successful orbit simply meant, "Okay, the company won't go bankrupt today." SpaceX could survive a little longer. In 2008, after the successful launch, while I was frantically raising funds for Tesla, NASA suddenly called to tell me that SpaceX had won their contract. It was unbelievable. I yelled, "I love NASA! You guys are amazing!" Then I hung up and called SpaceX President Gwen Shotwell, telling her that whatever NASA asked for, we'd sign immediately. This feeling of being saved was like being blindfolded and led to an execution squad. They yelled "Shoot!" I heard the click of the trigger, but the gun was empty, and then they let me go. I'm naturally very happy to have survived, but I'm still incredibly nervous. We're so lucky we didn't go bankrupt. At the time, NASA announced the retirement of the Space Shuttle, but they didn't have enough budget to develop a new spacecraft to transport cargo to the space station. They decided to hold a public tender—the first time in NASA's history. Our rocket ultimately cost about $6 million, much lower than other rockets in its class ($25 million). Our price was only a quarter of Boeing's or Lockheed's. Once rockets become reusable, payload transportation costs can be reduced by two orders of magnitude (to 1% of the original cost). What is the grand vision we want to achieve? The most ambitious optimization goal is to establish a city on Mars as quickly as possible. The next sub-goal is to build a fully functional rocket as quickly as possible. The next sub-goal after that is to send a spacecraft into orbit as quickly as possible. The initial production was just a trial run. The initial designs weren't intended for long-term use. We just wanted to accumulate experience as quickly as possible. To be honest, the early starship assembly area looked like a garage workshop. It's a rather absurd scene—we were actually working on cutting-edge technology in a tent in a parking lot. In one's life, there are bound to be some things that make you feel exhilarated and that your life has been worthwhile. For me, the most important thing is to get humans on Mars. Numerous challenges could lead to the end of civilization; they are the great filters for its survival. One of these is whether humanity can become a multiplanetary species, that is, to continue its civilization on multiple planets. Can humanity leave Earth and become one of the species to pass through this "great filter"? To become a multiplanetary species, we need a breakthrough: creating a rapidly reusable space transportation system. This is a formidable challenge, and the possibility of solving it is extremely slim. This is precisely the breakthrough that SpaceX is truly trying to achieve. Our current progress is good, better than expected. But this is only incremental, not revolutionary. We want to achieve a revolutionary technological leap.

data.title: SpaceX IPO Day 1 Strategy: Don't Treat It Like a Regular Hot Stock
data.description:
Original Title: SpaceX IPO Day: What Retail Traders Don't See (The Orderflow Read)

Original Author: The Flow Horse

Original Translation: Peggy, BlockBeats

Editor's Note: Against the backdrop of a mega-IPO, AI narratives, and repricing of risky assets, market discussions surrounding SpaceX's listing are shifting from "how much is this company actually worth" to "how it will be traded after its IPO." But as SpaceX becomes one of the most watched technology assets, a more crucial question begins to emerge: On the first day of trading for a new stock with no historical price, no mature option structure, and no clear distribution of shares, should investors understand it using a valuation framework or the market microstructure?

This article is compiled from The Flow Horse's video content on trading strategies for the first day of the SpaceX IPO. The focus is not on discussing SpaceX's long-term fundamentals, but rather on breaking down the potential challenges it faces in the early stages of its listing, including cash flow, free float, index inclusion, and the timing of share unlocking. The video's creator is a market trader who has long focused on IPOs and order flow trading; their perspective is closer to order book and trade execution than traditional company valuation analysis. In this content, the SpaceX IPO is broken down into a set of more fundamental structural issues: it's not a simple "should I buy?" question, but rather a process of repricing limited liquidity by traders, retail investors, passive funds, and insiders at different time windows. First, retail investors are most likely to misjudge the trading environment on the first day. In the past, retail investors typically relied on trend lines, support and resistance levels, previous highs and lows, and opening momentum when trading popular stocks. However, the SpaceX IPO had no historical charts, no areas of high trading volume, and no mature option structure on its first day. Before the first candlestick, the market had no reusable price memory. Now, what truly determines the short-term direction is the order book, trading volume, VWAP, the opening range, and where buyers and sellers actually exchange positions. This means that if retail investors chase the price in the first wave of the opening or prematurely use technical analysis to find so-called trends, they are likely to bear the highest risk before the structure has formed. Secondly, past popular IPOs do not support the notion that "there will inevitably be a one-sided rise in the early stages of listing." Coinbase, Airbnb, and ARM all had extremely high attention, but they did not immediately show a stable trend in their early listings; instead, they experienced violent two-way fluctuations. In the past, the market easily understood popular IPOs as the realization of emotional consensus; now, a more accurate understanding is that they often become a place where short-term funds, profit-taking, and new buying repeatedly change hands. This means that even with a strong narrative and high subscription rates, SpaceX's first week isn't necessarily suitable for trend followers. Those truly suited for the first day are traders who can quickly analyze order flow, manage positions, and accept two-way volatility. Third, the first-day strategy should shift from "predicting direction" to "waiting for structure." In the past, many traders were accustomed to setting bullish or bearish views before the market opened and then using the first wave of price action to validate their judgments. However, for a low-float IPO like SpaceX, it's even more crucial to let the market establish its structure first: is there support around $135? Has the 5-minute opening range been effectively broken? Has the VWAP retracement held? Are there constantly updated hidden buying and selling forces in Level 2? The core of trading now isn't about drawing conclusions before everyone else, but rather determining who holds the initiative after the market generates its first batch of price indicators. This means the most important thing isn't entering the market immediately, but avoiding being passively traded in the most chaotic, widest-spreading, and most emotionally charged positions. Fourth, investors must understand that different phases are driven by different types of capital. In the first 15 trading days, SpaceX resembles a short-term trade dominated by low float, sentimental capital, and order flow. Around the 15th trading day, the expectation of inclusion in the Nasdaq index may bring a second phase of price-insensitive buying. After the initial earnings report, the unlocking of supply begins to test market absorption. Further down the line, the unlocking of shares by major shareholders after 70, 90, 120, 180 days, and a year will gradually provide more reliable long-term signals. In the past, IPOs were often seen as determining success or failure based on the first day's price movement; now, SpaceX is more like a series of continuous liquidity tests. This means that long-term judgments should not be based on first-day sentiment, but rather on whether prices can form a stable bottom after the new supply enters the market. Fifth, the SpaceX transaction may not only occur within SpaceX itself. Aerospace and space economy-related stocks such as Rocket Lab and LUNR may become shadow stocks expressing the same theme during their listing period. In the past, IPO transactions typically revolved around the primary target company; now, when the primary target company has a low float, excessive volatility, and wide price spreads, related assets may offer a clearer trading structure. This means that the market is not only trading SpaceX stock, but also the industrial narrative and liquidity spillover it has activated. If we were to summarize this article into a single judgment: the first day of the SpaceX IPO belongs to traders, and long-term judgment depends on supply testing. For traders, the first day may be the "Super Bowl" of order flow trading; for investors, the first day's price fluctuations should not be over-interpreted. In this sense, the core issue of the SpaceX IPO is no longer just whether to buy on the first day, but whether participants can first determine which game they are entering: look at order flow on the first day, and look at supply absorption capacity in the long term. Confusing these two is precisely why most retail investors are most likely to lose money. The following is the video content (the original content has been edited for easier reading and understanding): Why Most Retail Investors Might Lose Money in the SpaceX IPO The most dangerous aspect of SpaceX's IPO day is that many people will treat it like a regular hot stock. Regular stocks have historical price ranges, previous highs and lows, areas of high trading volume, and ample market memory. Traders can refer to past support and resistance levels, moving averages, option positions, and cost of capital. But the first day of an IPO is a blank chart. Before the first candlestick appears, the market has no real trading history. This means that drawing trend lines too early is meaningless, and chasing the first wave of upward movement after the opening is easily broken by reverse fluctuations. Especially in a low-float environment, the price may surge rapidly due to short-term buying, or it may suddenly fall back due to profit-taking or institutional supply. Retail investors who only look at price increases and sentiment are easily misled into entering the market at the most chaotic points. The real trading logic on SpaceX's first day was the real-time formation of the auction mechanism (where buyers and sellers seek equilibrium at different prices). Traders needed to observe: Who was willing to raise prices to buy? At what price levels were sellers continuously replenishing their positions? Which price levels saw high trading volume but failed to push prices higher? This order book information was more important than any pre-drawn technical patterns. Trading Details: $75 Billion Financing, 3% Float Market Share, and High Retail Allocation. SpaceX's IPO planned to issue approximately 555 million shares, raising approximately $75 billion, priced at $135 per share, with an overall valuation of approximately $1.7 trillion. This scale alone was enough to make it a market-level event. However, what truly determines the volatility on the first day of trading is not just the scale of financing, but the circulating shares. Only about 3% of SpaceX's shares will be freely tradable in the initial listing period. This means that even moderate buying can significantly impact the price. Retail investors chasing the price, active funds building positions, and small-scale institutional buying can all cause the price to deviate from fundamentals in the short term. Another special variable is the retail allocation. The retail allocation ratio this time may reach about 30%, roughly 3 to 4 times that of a typical IPO. This will make trading after the opening more difficult to predict. On the one hand, more retail investors receiving shares in advance may reduce the impulse to chase the price on the first day of listing if they "can't buy"; on the other hand, these investors who received shares in advance may also take profits after the opening, forming the first wave of supply. Therefore, the core of SpaceX's IPO is not simply judging "oversubscription is good news," but understanding the shareholding structure: extremely low free float amplifies both rises and falls, while high retail allocations may make both buying and selling more aggressive on the first day. The 15th Trading Day: Nasdaq Inclusion May Change the Nature of Funds Another key time point is the 15th trading day after listing. According to its design, SpaceX may be included in the Nasdaq 100 Index (NDX). This arrangement is still subject to final rules and actual results, but its corresponding trading logic is very important. In the early stages of listing, the price is mainly driven by quick money, retail investors, active funds, and sentiment-driven funds. These funds are price-sensitive and will quickly enter and exit based on fluctuations. However, after index inclusion, the market will introduce another type of fund: passive funds. Passive funds are characterized by price-insensitive flow (meaning they must buy because of index rules or portfolio requirements, not because the price is cheap). Index funds, ETFs, and related tracking products need to allocate constituent stocks according to rules, and this buying is often more mechanical and easier for the market to trade in advance. Therefore, before the 15th trading day, active funds may attempt to "front-run" (buy before the expected buying pressure arrives). If SpaceX has already established upward momentum in the early stages of its listing, the mechanical buying brought about by index inclusion may further amplify the trend; however, if the performance is weak in the first two weeks, this buying may not be enough to reverse the market on its own. This is also what distinguishes the SpaceX IPO from ordinary first-day trading: it is not a single event, but a series of fund flow nodes. The first day is order flow trading, not chart trading. The most important judgment for SpaceX's first day is: don't treat it as chart trading. Ordinary traders are used to asking: Where is the support? Where is the resistance? Where is the previous high? Where is the area of ​​high trading volume? What are the biggest pain points in options? But on the first day of an IPO, most of these questions are unanswered. Without historical charts, there is no reliable technical structure; without a mature options market, there are no open interest contracts to refer to. The real questions on the first day are: Where do buyers and sellers reach an agreement? Where does a large turnover occur? After the price falls below the offering price, are there buyers willing to buy? When the price rises, are sellers continuously replenishing their positions? This is the core of order flow trading. The initial key price levels must be determined by the market itself on the first day. First is $135, the offering price shown in the video. Traders need to observe the price's performance relative to $135: can it quickly recover after falling below it? Can it sustain its position after rising above it? If there is buying support every time the price falls below $135, it indicates this level may become an early cost anchor; if there is selling pressure every time it rises above $135, it indicates stronger supply above. Next is VWAP (Volume-Weighted Average Price, representing the average transaction cost in the market that day). In the first hour after listing, whether the price is above or below VWAP, and whether it finds support when retracing to VWAP, directly reflects who has the upper hand. Finally, there are the first day's high and low. After the market closes, the high and low of the first day will become the most important structural references for the following days. For a newly listed stock without historical charts, the first day's price range is the market's first established coordinate system. The price fluctuations in SpaceX's IPO can be broken down into four types of funding flows. The first type is the scarcity buying driven by extremely low free float. A 3% free float means very few shares are available for trading, and even a slight concentration of demand can drive the stock price up rapidly. This is why blindly shorting on the first day is extremely dangerous. Low free float stocks may not rise indefinitely, but they are most likely to "crush" short sellers in a short period. The second type is the passive buying driven by inclusion in the Nasdaq index. If NDX is included on the 15th trading day, index funds and related products will need to buy according to the rules. These funds place orders based on index weights, not valuations. For bulls, this is an ideal mechanical demand; for short-term traders, it's a window of opportunity to trade in advance. The third type is option reflexivity (the options market influencing the underlying stock price). Once options are listed, a large influx of call options by retail investors could force market makers to buy underlying stock to hedge, creating a Gamma cycle (option buying drives market makers to buy underlying stock to hedge, further amplifying the price increase). However, this mechanism usually doesn't appear immediately on the first day of listing, and may not mature in the first week. The fourth type is share unlocks (restricted shares gradually entering the market). This introduces new supply and is a risk that all long-term investors must pay attention to. SpaceX is unique in that it doesn't simply wait 180 days for a one-time unlock, but may release shares in stages.

Unlocking Schedule: Not a One-Time Cliff Drop in 180 Days

A common risk in traditional IPOs is the sudden release of early investors' and employees' shares after the 180-day lock-up period, leading to a surge in market supply. However, the SpaceX unlocking structure presented in the video is more complex: it may not be a one-time cliff drop, but rather a series of phased liquidity events.

First, up to 20% of eligible shares may be unlocked two days after the initial earnings report. This means that the earnings report itself is not only a performance event but also a supply event. If the stock price is driven up by sentiment before the earnings report, the new supply after the report may suppress momentum.

Second, the unlocking may also be linked to price performance. If the stock price remains above $135 by more than 30% for five out of the ten trading days before the earnings report, an additional 10% may be unlocked.

This type of arrangement triggers more supply as the price rises, creating a dynamic equilibrium: the more sharply the price increases, the more shares may become available for sale later. Subsequent milestones are equally important. The video mentions that approximately 7% of shares may unlock on day 70, day 90, and day 120, with full unlocking after day 180. Regarding employee stock, approximately 5% of employee shares may be sold immediately after the first financial report, without additional performance or price conditions. Musk and the largest shareholder may need to wait more than a year, approximately 366 days. These dates are particularly important for long-term investors. Determining whether SpaceX has formed a true bottom cannot be based solely on the first day's price fluctuations, but rather on whether buying pressure can absorb each new supply entering the market. Lessons from Past IPOs: Coinbase, Airbnb, and ARM Didn't Start with a One-Sided Trend. Popular IPOs can easily create the illusion that high market attention guarantees a steady rise after listing. However, the early trading of Coinbase, Airbnb, and ARM demonstrates that hype does not equate to a one-sided trend. The video mentions that these popular IPOs all experienced significant volatility in their early stages. Coinbase's early volatility reached approximately 119 points, Airbnb's approximately 53 points, and ARM's approximately 22 points. The key point isn't the specific numbers themselves, but rather what they illustrate: the first few days and weeks of a popular IPO often see intense two-way trading, rather than a stable trend. This type of environment is more suitable for day scalpers (traders who profit by frequently entering and exiting small price swings in short periods) and order flow traders, rather than ordinary trend followers. Trend traders need structure, but that's precisely what's lacking in the early stages of an IPO. SpaceX might be even more extreme. It was already highly oversubscribed, and it's possible that even more retail investors will receive their allocations early. This means that after the opening, there will be both funds chasing the rally and funds taking profits; some want to buy in anticipation of the index being included in the 15-day timeframe, while others believe that the high level of hype itself is a selling opportunity. With both bullish and bearish forces crowding in simultaneously, the result is often not a clean trend, but rather high turnover, high volatility, and high noise. Day 1 Trading Strategy: Wait for the market to establish a structure before taking action. The first rule for trading SpaceX on its first day is not to chase the first wave at the opening. The opening bell is typically the noisiest, most volatile, and most emotionally charged time. Especially in a low-float environment, the first upward move might just be a short-lived sell-off, and the first downward move might simply be a sharp drop due to insufficient liquidity. A truly tradable structure requires the market to form. The first point to observe is $135. If the price falls below $135 and then quickly recovers, regaining its position above the opening range and VWAP, it indicates genuine support below. Conversely, if the price repeatedly attempts to rise above $135 but is sold off, it suggests that sellers may be in control. The second point to observe is the 5-minute opening range (the high and low range formed in the first 5 minutes after the opening). Using the 5-minute or 30-minute opening range to filter out noise can prevent premature stop-loss triggers. If the opening range is narrow, a subsequent breakout is more meaningful because a large number of short-term positions are concentrated within a limited price range. Once the price leaves the range, stop-loss orders and chasing orders will be more easily triggered. The third point to observe is the VWAP. An hour after listing, if the price is trading above the VWAP and quickly finds buying support when it retraces to the VWAP, it may be a signal that the bulls are in control. If the price remains below the VWAP for a long time with weak rebounds, it indicates that the average transaction cost for the day is becoming resistance. The fourth point to observe is the "Ghost Level" (i.e., a position in the order book where hidden buying and selling forces are continuously absorbing trading volume). If a price level appears to have small order books, but trading volume continues to increase, yet the price fails to break through, it indicates that there may be hidden sellers continuously replenishing their positions. Conversely, if a low level sees continuous trading but the price doesn't fall, it may also indicate hidden buyers absorbing the position. These temporary price levels may not be meaningful for the next few months, but they are crucial on the first day. Because without historical charts, these are the first roadmaps the market has just generated. Position Control and Risk Management: Don't Trade Low-Frequency New Stocks as Blue-Chip Stocks. SpaceX may be a large company, but its initial trading characteristics may not resemble those of large blue-chip stocks. If the free float is only 3%, the order book may be thin, the bid-ask spread may be wide, and the price may fluctuate rapidly due to continuous sweeps. Furthermore, the options market may not be mature in its early stages, and traders lack hedging tools; excessively large positions can easily amplify losses due to slippage and trading halt risks. Therefore, SpaceX cannot be traded using the position sizing methods used for mature large-cap stocks like Apple, Microsoft, and Nvidia. Beginners who only want to learn order flow should start with very small positions, or even just observe without trading. For most people, missing the first day's opportunity isn't the worst problem; the biggest risk is being driven by FOMO to heavily invest in the most chaotic positions. Short-term traders need to accept the reality that the most important thing on the first day isn't to profit from every fluctuation, but to avoid being "washed out" by the market in unstructured positions. Long-term investors should focus more on subsequent supply milestones, rather than rushing to prove they were right about SpaceX on the first day of listing. Four things you must understand: First, the first 15 trading days are highly likely to be driven by sentiment. Extremely low float, huge attention, and a lack of technical benchmarks will mean that prices will primarily rely on order flow and short-term funds. Second, the 15th trading day could be a turning point in the nature of capital flows. If SpaceX is included in the Nasdaq 100, price-insensitive mechanical buying may enter the market, and active funds may also trade in anticipation of this. Third, the initial earnings report is not only a performance event but also a point of supply pressure. Some shares may begin to unlock after the earnings report, and the market needs to prove its ability to absorb the increased supply. Fourth, the unlocking is not a one-off event but rather a series of liquidity tests throughout the next year. The unlocking of shares after 70 days, 90 days, 120 days, 180 days, and even a year later by major shareholders, will all re-examine the true buying base of SpaceX. **SpaceX-Related Stocks Worth Watching** During SpaceX's IPO, related industry proxy stocks (alternatives used to represent similar themes or risk exposures when the core asset cannot be directly traded) are also worth watching. Stocks related to aerospace, defense, rocket launches, satellites, and the space economy may benefit from capital spillover due to SpaceX's market popularity. Examples mentioned in the video include Rocket Lab (RKLB) and LUNR, among other space-related stocks. The opportunity for these proxy stocks lies not in their guaranteed benefit, but in their potential to become alternative pathways for capital to express the SpaceX theme. Especially when SpaceX itself has a wide price range, high volatility, and low free float, some more liquid related stocks may offer clearer trading structures. However, proxy trading must be verified through market activity. If the relevant stocks only experience a brief surge without significant trading volume, sustainability, or relative strength, then the SpaceX logic cannot be forcibly applied.

data.title: The biggest obstacle to tokenizing US stocks is disappearing.
data.description:
Source:Galaxy Research Director Alex Thorn

Translation: Kaozai

The SEC has just proposed repealing Reg NMS Rule 611—this "transaction crossing rule" has defined the structure of the U.S. stock market since 2005.

While this is a traditional finance story, it is also the most significant policy deregulation to date regarding tokenized stocks.

This is certainly a traditional finance story, but it is also the most significant policy deregulation to date regarding tokenized stocks.

The SEC has voted to repeal Rule 611 (order protection rule) and Rule 610(e) (lock-up/cross-market restrictions), along with their definitions. A 60-day public comment period is open. It is currently a proposal and not yet finalized—but the policy direction is clear. Rule 611 requires each exchange to execute trades at a price that is not inferior to the protected quote displayed on other exchanges. In practice, every trade of any NMS stock must reference and adhere to the National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) at the time of execution. This is one of the biggest structural obstacles facing tokenized U.S. stock trading in DeFi today. Automated Market Makers (AMMs) are inherently unable to comply with Rule 611 by design—they execute transactions along a bonding curve at the block time granularity, resulting in slippage, and the execution price depends on the pool price. AMMs cannot send intermarket sweep orders, cannot access SIP data with latency guarantees, and cannot abort a swap due to a better offer on Nasdaq. Any liquidity pool for tokenized NMS shares will continuously generate "trade crossings," potentially constituting an illegal trading center under legal regulations. The same applies to Rule 610(e). AMM prices will continuously drift with fund flows and frequently lock or cross over displayed NBBOs, practices explicitly prohibited by all current trading venues. So, what will replace Section 611 after its repeal? The answer is best execution. This obligation falls at the brokerage level (FINRA 5310) and is principle-oriented, rather than a mandatory requirement for each trade. Brokerages route orders to on-chain liquidity pools and can fulfill this obligation through periodic reviews. This framework can accommodate AMMs—something the old framework could never do. Tokenized NMS shares still face many other issues, including exchange/ATS registration, clearing and settlement, and a large number of regulations not designed for DeFi or peer-to-peer trading. We hope that many of these issues will be addressed in the SEC's upcoming "innovation exemption" framework. From a macro perspective, this reflects the SEC's implementation of its "crypto project roadmap": removing the most insurmountable market structural barriers by repealing the rule, and then addressing venue registration issues (at least temporarily) through innovation exemptions. This policy sequence is crucial. The stock market structure for the past two decades has been built around a single rule, and now the SEC is repealing it. This is a significant step in clearing obstacles for the next phase of innovation in stock and securities trading. Another important background point is worth adding. When current Chairman Atkins was a commissioner in the early 2000s, he voted against the passage of Reg NMS, and yesterday's proposed repeal almost perfectly aligns with his objections from that time. In June 2005, Atkins and Commissioner Cynthia Glassman co-signed a 44-page written objection to the 3-2 vote on Reg NMS. They argued that Congress intended for competition (not regulation) to shape the national market system, and that Section 611 effectively replaced the market itself with the SEC's subjective judgment of the optimal market structure. Their empirical arguments were extremely compelling. SEC research showed that, measured by actual volume, "trading slippage" was only a 1% to 2% phenomenon. The estimated loss was $321 million, while the annual trading volume during the same period reached $16.8 trillion—which they called a "negligible rounding error." They also predicted that Section 611 would not channel liquidity to the open market, but instead incentivize traders to hide large orders and keep them secret. The reality today is that the SEC's proposal yesterday cited data from its own staff showing that over-the-counter trading volume has reached 51.9% of Nasdaq-listed stocks and 47% of NYSE-listed stocks. Even within exchanges, the median share of trading volume targeting hidden orders has nearly doubled, climbing from 16% in 2015 to over 30% in 2025. The SEC's own accumulated regulatory data now confirms the core predictions made during the initial objections. So what was their alternative in 2005? Improved quote accessibility, enhanced interoperability, and reliance on brokers' best execution obligations rather than government oversight of every transaction. This is precisely the framework the SEC proposed yesterday. The proposal even directly references that 2005 objection. The proposal also explicitly points to its relevance to the crypto industry. It specifically discusses tokenized securities and "smart contracts that underpin automated market makers," and cites a paper that argues that it is mandatory rules like Section 611 that have prevented the stock market from developing AMMs, intents, or atomic settlement mechanisms. So this isn't deregulation for the sake of deregulation. This is the SEC chairman putting his own dissenting arguments into practice 21 years later—and data from SEC staff now corroborates his 2005 claims. Regardless of your stance on this proposal, its administrative record is solid. This isn't just about tokenized securities; the current SEC believes the original legislative basis for Section 611 was never sufficient, anticipates it will hinder rather than promote market development, and has empirical evidence to back it up. Of course, it also benefits tokenized securities—as I said, timing is equally crucial. Finally, it's important to note that this didn't happen suddenly. Back in July 2025, the SEC announced a roundtable discussion on this issue, explicitly stating that "Reg NMS and its Section 611 have not benefited investors or brokerages; on the contrary, they have distorted the market and been exploited by various parties through game theory..." Subsequently, the SEC held two public roundtable discussions (September in Washington, D.C., and December at the University of Austin), widely soliciting opinions before submitting this proposal. The entire process was not a hasty decision, but rather the result of meticulous planning and thorough prior notice. The entire industry had the opportunity to express its opinions at every stage. And now, the market can still submit opinions on this proposal.

data.title: Musk sits at the table, the world bets.
data.description:
$970 billion. This is Musk's latest net worth. He's just one step away from becoming the first trillionaire in human history. SpaceX just completed its IPO at $135 per share, raising $75 billion and reaching a valuation of $1.77 trillion. It immediately became the seventh largest listed company in the US, with a market capitalization exceeding JPMorgan Chase, Berkshire Hathaway, Meta, and even Musk's own Tesla. Why is a company with accumulated losses exceeding $13 billion worth this much? A Rewritten IPO Script Let's look at how SpaceX went public. A normal company IPO involves a roadshow: investment banks take the CEO around the world to meet investors, set a price range, listen to feedback, and gradually refine the process. This is industry practice, and the core purpose is to ensure that the market price is as close as possible to the company's true value. Musk didn't need this process. $135, a fixed price, no negotiation. He allocated a whopping 30% of the shares directly to retail investors, which is almost unheard of in large IPOs. The retail allocation ratio for most companies is usually below 5%, but SpaceX increased it five or six times.

More covertly, this involves operations at the rule level.

According to reports, Nasdaq has opened a "fast track" for SpaceX's inclusion in the index.What does this mean?

Passive funds tracking major indices like the Nasdaq 100 (such as ETFs like QQQ) will be forced to allocate shares of SpaceX proportionally after its inclusion in the index—regardless of the fund manager's own opinion. Industry estimates suggest this could result in a passive capital inflow of between $10 billion and $20 billion. Whether passive funds buy or not is not determined by the fund manager, but by the index rules. Under this mechanism, SpaceX receives a layer of "institutional liquidity protection." Meanwhile, while Musk holds approximately 42% of the economic equity, through a super-voting rights structure (one share, multiple votes), he controls a staggering 82%-84% of the voting rights. Your funds are in, but the decision-making power remains firmly in his hands. When someone becomes powerful enough, the rules will automatically change to suit them. This isn't a moral judgment, but an observation of the realities of power. The $1.77 trillion narrative framework supports this valuation through a sophisticated story package. SpaceX has bundled three businesses together: Starlink (a relatively mature and profitable business), X (social media), and xAI (the AI ​​sector, currently still burning through cash). Selling these three as a package tells the story of a "space AI computing empire." The prospectus lists the total addressable market (TAM) at a staggering $28.5 trillion—a figure exceeding China's total GDP. Using a country's economic scale to endorse a company's potential is a narrative technique that should be viewed with caution. The financial reality is that it lost $4.3 billion in the first quarter of 2025, and has accumulated losses of $13 billion since the beginning of 2023. Its revenue during the same period was $4.7 billion. For reference, Meta's first-quarter revenue was $56.3 billion, and its market capitalization was $1.4 trillion. SpaceX's revenue is less than one-tenth of Meta's, yet its valuation is more than 25% higher. After the merger, xAI's valuation soared to $1.25 trillion; it acquired AI programming company Cursor for $60 billion; and it leased computing power to Anthropic and Google, generating monthly revenue of up to $2.17 billion. Space AI data centers, lunar robotic factories, Mars settlement plans—even clauses like "unlocking $600 billion in salaries for Mars colonists" are written into the prospectus. The line between vision and empty promises is sometimes just a single `.execute()` function. Musk has indeed delivered many "impossible" things in the past, but that doesn't mean he will this time. A Sober Reflection on Timing: Why Go Public Now? AI giants like OpenAI are also queuing up for IPOs. SpaceX's early move is essentially a liquidity race at the peak of the tech bubble—whoever gets there first reaps the biggest profits. This is a classic example of first-mover advantage. Another detail: the SEC's speed in pushing forward this IPO was unusually rapid, despite public protests from pension funds. A familiar pattern—Tesla also used the "list first, then issue the ticket" model. The essence of regulatory arbitrage is to exploit the time lag in rule enforcement to cash out before constraints arrive. This isn't unique to SpaceX, but SpaceX has perfected this model. The profit chain at the table: Behind this IPO lies a clear profit distribution diagram. Musk holds approximately 42% of the shares, and the post-IPO path is clear: pledge shares to cash out and sell at a high price. Who bears the risk? Retail investors and passive funds. Early investors (Founders Fund, a16z, Sequoia, etc.) will exit with a liquidity premium, realizing their bets from the past two decades in one go. Who transfers the risk? Secondary market buyers. The 21 underwriting investment banks (Goldman Sachs firmly holds the lead underwriter position, with the remaining 20 vying for a share) are estimated to earn over $1 billion in commissions. They face virtually no risk in this chain—a guaranteed income regardless of market conditions. An interesting phenomenon is that after a project concludes, key investment banking personnel are often poached by SpaceX or competitors, creating a "revolving door" between investment banks and publicly traded companies. Today's underwriters become tomorrow's executives; this bond of interest is stronger than any contract. To put it bluntly, this scheme is ingeniously designed. Early players are responsible for storytelling and bearing the initial risks, Wall Street provides endorsement and distribution, index funds are forced to provide liquidity, and retail investors are responsible for buying in and fueling their faith. Everyone knows the rules of the game, but not everyone realizes which seat they're in. Both perspectives are worth listening to. SpaceX's rocket business is unparalleled globally—over 80% of the rockets launched into orbit in the past three years were launched by SpaceX. Starlink covers 164 countries and is a genuine commercial infrastructure. These assets are real, and its technological leadership is real. The problem is, real assets and real bubbles can coexist. Tesla's history has already played out a similar script: its valuation was questioned, short sellers were squeezed, and it ultimately grew into a trillion-dollar company amidst controversy. Will SpaceX repeat this? Possibly. But another possibility exists: $1.77 trillion is already the peak, and the story that follows is a long process of value correction. Those who believe in Musk see a record of continuously delivering miracles. Skeptics see 13 billion in accumulated losses, a narrative bubble of 28.5 trillion TAM, and passive funds locked up by the system. Both perspectives have factual basis, and neither can be simply dismissed. However, one thing is certain: when the market replaces pricing models with faith, prices can deviate from fundamentals for a long time—but "a long time" is not "forever." The market has already cast its vote with real money, and time will tell. SpaceX's $1.77 trillion price tag—do you think it's a reasonable price for the space age, or just another familiar scenario?

data.title: Besides Musk, SpaceX has quietly created nine more billionaires.
data.description:
With SpaceX launching its IPO at a $1.8 trillion valuation, Elon Musk's wealth has soared to unprecedented heights. Meanwhile, a CFO who managed his accounts for 14 years, a female executive who rose from employee number 11 to president, a Twitter co-founder, and a Saudi prince who made an early bet on the space investment boom have also reaped enormous fortunes in this space-capital feast. SpaceX's record-breaking IPO pricing has brought Elon Musk one step closer to becoming a trillionaire. The listing has created a new billionaire and significantly increased the wealth of numerous investors. SpaceX set its IPO price at $135 per share on Thursday, June 11, giving the company a total market capitalization of $1.8 trillion. Forbes estimates that this will increase Elon Musk's net worth by $188 billion, bringing his total net worth to approximately $982 billion. If Tesla's stock price remains unchanged, and SpaceX's stock rises to $138.50 per share after Friday's opening, Musk will become the first trillionaire in human history. If Tesla's stock price rises from $399 to $424, and SpaceX's stock price remains unchanged at $135 (a less likely scenario), Musk will also become a trillionaire. Elon Musk serves as Chairman, CEO, and CTO of SpaceX, holding 4.8 billion shares of the aerospace company, worth $644 billion. In addition, he holds 350 million stock options with an exercise price of $8.4 per share, corresponding to a value of $44 billion. Musk holds a total of 38% of the company's shares, with a total value of $688 billion based on the IPO price. In February of this year, SpaceX merged with Elon Musk's artificial intelligence and social media company, xAI (xAI will merge with Twitter's predecessor, X, in March 2025), at which time the overall valuation was $1.25 trillion. Based on this, Forbes estimated at the time that Musk's approximately 40% stake (not yet diluted by the IPO) was worth about $500 billion. Musk also holds over 10% of Tesla's shares, which have a market capitalization of $1.5 trillion, worth $165 billion. With his stock options, he could also purchase nearly 8% of Tesla shares, worth $114 billion. In addition, his wealth includes minority stakes in brain-computer interface company Neuralink and tunneling company The Boring Company, as well as billions of dollars in proceeds from the sale of Tesla stock in his early years. Forbes' latest estimate of Musk's net worth did not include performance-linked restricted stock. If certain conditions are met, Musk's stakes in SpaceX and Tesla will rise to 47% and 29% respectively (before deducting taxes and costs associated with unlocking restricted stock). To acquire all of these shares, Musk needs to achieve a series of challenging goals: increasing the market capitalization of SpaceX and Tesla to $7.5 trillion and $8.5 trillion respectively, and establishing a permanent human settlement on Mars with a population of at least 1 million. Musk is undoubtedly the biggest winner of this IPO, but he is not the only one whose wealth has skyrocketed. A Saudi prince, the co-founder of Twitter, and several SpaceX executives and board members have all reaped substantial rewards. Brett Johnson, the long-serving CFO, has become a new member of the billionaire club, holding 0.07% of the company's shares (including options), with an estimated net worth of $1.2 billion. Previously, when the aerospace company filed its IPO prospectus, it also disclosed another newly minted billionaire: Luke Nosek. This venture capitalist is the co-founder of PayPal, a long-time investor in SpaceX, and a member of the company's board of directors. Nosek's stake is at least 0.19%, worth at least $3.4 billion. The prospectus also shows that Gwen Shotwell, SpaceX's longtime president and COO, currently holds 0.10% of the company, lower than Forbes' previous estimate of 0.27%. Even so, this 11th employee of the company is now worth $1.7 billion. Among the beneficiaries of this IPO, many were originally investors in X or xAI, including Saudi Prince Alwaleed bin Talal Al Saud and US President Trump's Middle East envoy, Steven Witkov. The two became investors in SpaceX in February of this year, when Musk merged the aerospace company with his artificial intelligence and social media entity, xAI Holdings, creating a combined valuation of $1.25 trillion. In the February deal, SpaceX was valued at $1 trillion and xAI Holdings at $250 billion. Less than a year ago, in March 2025, Musk merged the artificial intelligence startup xAI with the original Twitter X to form xAI Holdings, valued at $113 billion. Many critics believe this move was actually a bailout for the X platform. The reason is that the transaction valued X at $33 billion, almost the same as the amount paid by Musk and minority shareholders such as Prince Alwaleed and Witkov to acquire the platform in 2022 (excluding debt financing). Meanwhile, Fidelity's valuation of the X platform at the end of 2024 was less than $10 billion. Other notable beneficiaries include several well-known venture capitalists, hedge fund managers, and private equity billionaires whose firms bet on SpaceX years ago. Among them is Antonio Gracias, a close friend of Elon Musk and a SpaceX board member; his company, Valor Equity Partners, holds a 3.7% stake in the company, worth $68 billion.
